title: ZKP-Enforced Agent Key Rotation Without Human Approver
description: On Theseus, when an agent's EdDSA key is compromised or reaches its rotation epoch, there is no human fallback to authorize a new key. A ZKP-based rotation proof allows the agent to attest it holds both the old private key and the new one — committing to a key-transition nullifier on-chain — without revealing either key. This is structurally different from standard key rotation (which requires a human CA or multisig owner): the proof itself is the authorization. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit can be extended to include a rotation witness, and the IdentityRegistry contract can enforce that a valid ZKP is the only valid rotation path. Evidence: Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint makes this a day-1 operational gap — raw EdDSA rotation on an L1 with no human approver means a compromised key stays live until the agent self-destructs.

rationale.agent_need: Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint creates a structural day-1 gap where a compromised EdDSA key stays live indefinitely without a self-sovereign rotation mechanism, confirmed by the architectural constraint itself.
rationale.zkp_edge: Lit Protocol requires an external threshold network (liveness dependency), Turnkey requires operator authorization, and raw on-chain EdDSA rotation has no way to prove key continuity without revealing the old private key — ZKP is the only mechanism that proves simultaneous possession of both keys without exposure, though the gap over MPC alternatives needs stronger evidence.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness nullifier pattern, and IdentityRegistry's nullifierMap all provide reusable components, but a new AgentRotation.circom circuit requires 3+ days of new Circom development plus PLONK setup, and the spec assumes pot16.ptau is sufficient without verifying constraint counts for the double-EdDSA witness.
rationale.partnership_leverage: A new Circom circuit with double-EdDSA witness and trusted setup is non-trivial ZKP engineering that Theseus would need months to build, but the evidence doesn't rule out them adopting Lit Protocol's MPC approach as a faster alternative, weakening the exclusivity argument.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (not live-fetched — web search blocked this session)
evidence.summary: Context confirms Theseus enforces a 'no human key path' constraint — agents hold raw EdDSA keypairs with no CA or multisig fallback, making unauthorized-key-persistence the day-1 operational gap this opportunity targets.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol — litprotocol.com (PKP + Lit Actions, known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Lit's Programmable Key Pairs use threshold MPC (not ZKP proofs) for autonomous key operations; rotation is policy-gated via Lit Actions but requires the threshold network as an external trust anchor — a liveness dependency that a self-contained ZKP proof avoids.

evidence.source: Turnkey — turnkey.com (HSM-backed key management, known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Turnkey provides policy-engine-gated key operations backed by AWS Nitro enclaves; rotation is operator-authorized, not agent-self-authorized — incompatible with Theseus's no-human-approver constraint.

evidence.source: Privy — privy.io (embedded wallet infrastructure, known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Privy is user-wallet-centric with social recovery tied to human accounts; structurally mismatched to agent-native chains with no human root.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core / DID:Key spec + IETF draft-ietf-oauth-dpop
evidence.summary: Existing standards assume key rotation is authorized by the DID controller (a human or org); no standard currently defines a ZKP-only rotation proof path — Bolyra's proposed mechanism would be novel relative to the spec landscape.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai AEA framework + Cosmos DID registry (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai agents use CosmWasm-based DID documents for identity; key rotation requires on-chain governance tx signed by the current key — no ZKP path, and human upgrade authority is implicit in validator governance.

evidence.source: SingularityNET AGIX — on-chain agent registry (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: SingularityNET's agent marketplace uses Ethereum-signed registrations; no dedicated key rotation or ZKP identity layer for autonomous agent credentials.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 (Ethereum) — on-chain nullifier-based identity (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Semaphore demonstrates the nullifier commitment pattern Bolyra already reuses for HumanUniqueness; extending the same pattern to a key-transition nullifier (old_key_commit → new_key_commit with double-spend guard) is architecturally straightforward given Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit.

evidence.source: Polygon ID / iden3 protocol — ZKP credentials on EVM (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Polygon ID supports ZKP-verified credential updates but targets human DIDs; no agent-native rotation primitive without a human issuer in the trust chain.

evidence.source: Aztec Protocol — private L2 with ZKP state transitions (known prior to cutoff)
evidence.summary: Aztec's private note model could represent key-state transitions, but it operates as an L2 app layer — not a reusable rotation primitive portable to Theseus's L1.

mapped_primitives.usage: The EdDSA operator signature verification logic is the direct ancestor of the rotation witness. AgentPolicy already verifies that an agent holds the private key corresponding to a credential commitment via EdDSA over Baby Jubjub. For rotation, the same gate can be instantiated twice — once for the old key (proving current registry membership) and once for the new key (committing to the successor credential). The scope commitment output (Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp)) provides the chain-link between old and new credential state.

The nullifier pattern — Poseidon2(scope, secret) — is borrowed directly as the design pattern for the rotation nullifier. For key rotation, the analogous construction is Poseidon2(rotationEpoch, oldCredentialCommitment), binding the one-time-use nullifier to a specific rotation epoch so the same old key cannot authorize two different successor keys. No modification to this circuit; it supplies the cryptographic idiom.

The scope-narrowing chain-linking logic (identity-bound scope commitment chain, expiry narrowing) is structurally reusable for rotation continuity: the new key's credential commitment must carry forward all permission constraints from the old one, preventing a rotation event from silently escalating permissions. The delegatee expiry <= delegator expiry invariant maps cleanly to newExpiry <= oldExpiry as a rotation constraint. No circuit modification needed — the logical pattern is extracted, not the circuit itself.

The agentTree (LeanIMT + 30-entry root history buffer) is the on-chain state that rotation must update atomically. The existing delegation nullifier replay protection mechanism is the template for rotation nullifier tracking. The 'atomic handshake verification in a single tx' pattern extends to an 'atomic rotation verification' call: verify rotation proof, check nullifier unused, update agentTree leaf, emit RotationCompleted event. Requires a new entry point function but the storage layout and nullifier map infrastructure already exists.

The rotation circuit should target PLONK (same as AgentPolicy and Delegation) to avoid a project-specific Groth16 trusted setup ceremony. The existing PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db will be reused as the on-chain verifier for rotation proofs, eliminating a new verifier deployment if the circuit's verification key is registered to the same verifier contract.

new_components_needed.description: AgentKeyRotation.circom — PLONK circuit with private inputs: oldEdDSAPrivKey, newEdDSAPrivKey, oldCredentialCommitment, rotationEpoch, oldMerklePathElements[20], oldMerklePathIndices[20]. Public outputs: oldCredentialCommitment (for registry lookup), newCredentialCommitment (Poseidon3 of same permissions + new pubkey + newExpiry), rotationNullifier (Poseidon2(rotationEpoch, oldCredentialCommitment)), newLeafHash. Constraints: two EdDSA Baby Jubjub key derivations, two Poseidon3 commitment checks, one LeanIMT membership proof on old leaf, one nullifier derivation, one permission-preservation check asserting newPermissionBitmask == oldPermissionBitmask AND newExpiry <= oldExpiry. Estimated ~24K constraints, within pot16.ptau budget.

IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey(rotationProof, publicSignals) entry point — verifies PLONK rotation proof via PlonkVerifier, reads oldCredentialCommitment from publicSignals and confirms it is a current agentTree leaf (using root history buffer for recency tolerance), checks rotationNullifier not consumed (new mapping: rotationNullifiers), updates agentTree by replacing old leaf with newLeafHash, stores rotation event with epoch for audit trail. Also adds a rotationEpoch admin setter (operator-controlled, Theseus can call via their bridge contract).

SDK v0.3 rotation API: createRotationCredential(oldAgentCredential, newOperatorKey, rotationEpoch) → AgentCredential; proveRotation(oldCredential, newCredential, rotationEpoch) → RotationProof; submitRotation(proof, registryAddress) → RotationReceipt. New error classes: KeyRotationNullifierUsedError, RotationPermissionEscalationError, RotationExpiryEscalationError, StaleAgentRootError.

Theseus chain bridge adapter — thin contract or off-chain relay that listens for Theseus-native key-compromise events (or epoch triggers) and initiates the Bolyra rotation flow on Base Sepolia. Two deployment models: (A) off-chain relay: Theseus agent SDK calls @bolyra/sdk directly, submits rotation proof to Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry, then posts the Base Sepolia tx hash back to Theseus as proof-of-rotation; (B) on-chain bridge: a lightweight Theseus-deployed contract that accepts a rotation receipt from Base Sepolia via LayerZero or Axelar and gates agent operations until rotation is confirmed. Model A is the 2-week sprint target; model B is v0.4.

Key rotation profile for draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 — addendum section defining rotationEpoch semantics, rotation nullifier construction, permission-preservation invariants, and the prohibition on human-path bypass. Required for Theseus to reference a normative spec rather than an implementation detail.

theseus_integration_surface: Primary surface is the IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey() function on Base Sepolia (0x2781dF8b6381462d881C833Fb703d68c661c9577 + new entry point post-upgrade). Theseus agents invoke @bolyra/sdk's proveRotation() locally — the proving happens client-side in ~16s (PLONK, same order as AgentPolicy) — then submit the calldata to Base Sepolia via a standard EIP-1559 tx. Theseus has no native Base Sepolia awareness, so the integration shim is an off-chain relay process (Node.js, ships as @bolyra/theseus-adapter) that: (1) subscribes to Theseus's epoch-clock or compromise-signal events, (2) calls @bolyra/sdk to generate the rotation proof, (3) submits to IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia, (4) posts the resulting tx hash + rotationNullifier back to Theseus as an off-chain attestation the agent can present. For Theseus to enforce on-chain that only ZKP-rotated agents can operate, they would deploy a thin BolyraRotationGate.sol on their chain that calls a Bolyra-maintained storage oracle or accepts the Base Sepolia tx receipt verified via their chosen bridge (LayerZero being the most operator-friendly given Bolyra's Base deployment). The SDK bridge is the 2-week deliverable; the on-chain gate is v0.4.

deliverables.name: AgentRotation.circom
deliverables.description: New Circom 2 circuit extending AgentPolicy with a double-key witness: proves simultaneous possession of old EdDSA private key (current registry member) and new EdDSA private key (successor), outputs rotation nullifier Poseidon2(rotationEpoch, oldCredentialCommitment) and new scope commitment, enforces newExpiry <= oldExpiry and no permission escalation via cumulative-bit comparison. Targets PLONK to reuse pot16.ptau and avoid a new Groth16 ceremony.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy (double-instantiated EdDSA gate + scope commitment), HumanUniqueness (nullifier construction idiom), Delegation (expiry narrowing constraint)

deliverables.name: AgentRotation PLONK trusted setup + vkey
deliverables.description: Run snarkjs plonk setup against pot16.ptau for AgentRotation.circom, export rotation.zkey and rotation.vkey.json, verify constraint count stays under 2^16. Check if existing PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db accepts the new vkey or needs a new verifier deployment.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey()
deliverables.description: New entry point on IdentityRegistry: accepts (rotationProof, publicSignals) — verifies PLONK proof, checks rotation nullifier not in nullifierMap, atomically updates agentTree leaf from old to new credential commitment, stores nullifier, emits RotationCompleted(agentId, oldCommitment, newCommitment, epoch). Reuses existing nullifierMap storage layout and LeanIMT update pattern from delegation flow. Includes Hardhat integration test: rotation succeeds, replay fails, permission-escalation proof rejected.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry (nullifierMap, agentTree LeanIMT, atomic verification pattern)

deliverables.name: SDK rotateAgentKey() method
deliverables.description: New public method in @bolyra/sdk: rotateAgentKey(oldCredential, newOperatorPrivKey, rotationEpoch) → generates AgentRotation witness, runs PLONK prove via snarkjs (or rapidsnark subprocess), returns { rotationProof, publicSignals, newCredential }. Caller submits to IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey() on-chain. Type-safe, matches existing createAgentCredential return shape so downstream code (proveHandshake) needs zero changes.

deliverables.name: End-to-end rotation demo script
deliverables.description: Single Node script: enroll agent with old key → generate rotation proof → submit on-chain → verify old credential rejected by IdentityRegistry → generate handshake with new credential → verify succeeds. Runs against local Hardhat node. Output is a clean terminal trace showing nullifier recorded, agentTree root updated, handshake passing. This is what gets screen-shared on the Theseus call once built.
deliverables.reuses: existing test harness (examples/mcp-demo, Hardhat deploy scripts)

deliverables.name: Theseus integration architecture spec
deliverables.description: One-page technical spec for the Sunday call: circuit I/O diagram, rotation nullifier construction, IdentityRegistry call sequence, EVM deployment assumptions, and 3 open questions requiring Theseus input (VM compatibility, agent lifecycle event triggers, rotation epoch definition). Not a full IETF draft — purpose is to demonstrate concrete thinking and open a scoped technical conversation.

spec.demo_description: Sunday call shows: (1) live walkthrough of existing AgentPolicy.circom as the direct ancestor — the EdDSA gate that will be doubled in AgentRotation; (2) HumanUniqueness nullifier code as the rotation nullifier template; (3) IdentityRegistry nullifierMap storage as the replay-protection substrate; (4) the one-page architecture spec with circuit I/O and call sequence; (5) a rough constraint-count estimate proving pot16.ptau (2^16) is sufficient. Goal is to establish Bolyra has 60% of the primitives already and needs 8 days to close the gap — not to show running code.
risks: AgentRotation constraint count may exceed 2^16 (pot16.ptau limit) if the double-EdDSA witness is expensive — mitigation: benchmark witness size against AgentPolicy baseline before writing full circuit, and have pot17.ptau fallback ready
Theseus VM may not be EVM-compatible — if they run a custom CosmWasm or SVM-derivative, IdentityRegistry.sol cannot deploy there and the integration requires a proof-relay bridge, adding 3-5 days
PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99 may not support dynamic vkey registration — if it's a static verifier, a new verifier contract must be deployed and the solo-founder timeline tightens

Rotation epoch semantics undefined — without Theseus confirming how rotation epochs are defined and emitted on-chain, the circuit's epoch binding is architecturally sound but untestable on their actual chain
8-day estimate assumes no other fires (autoresearch loops, protocol-autoresearch, SDK maintenance) — any context switch extends to 12 days
spec.success_criteria: A rotation proof generated by @bolyra/sdk passes IdentityRegistry.rotateAgentKey() on-chain verification, the old credential commitment's nullifier is stored (replay attempt reverts), the agentTree Merkle root updates atomically, and a subsequent proveHandshake() call using the new credential succeeds while the same call with the old credential fails — all demonstrated in a single deterministic Hardhat integration test.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) VM confirmation: EVM-compatible or custom (determines if IdentityRegistry.sol deploys natively vs requires a proof-relay bridge). 2) Testnet RPC + faucet access before end of sprint. 3) Current agent identity encoding: how EdDSA public keys are stored on-chain today (raw bytes32? DID document? compressed Baby Jubjub point?). 4) Rotation epoch definition: is it time-based, block-based, or event-triggered, and who emits it? 5) Agent lifecycle ownership: does Theseus have an existing AgentRegistry contract we must integrate with, or is Bolyra's IdentityRegistry the proposed registry?

tier3_findings: AXIS 2 FATAL: Standard EdDSA rotation (old_private_key signs new_public_key → on-chain state update) never exposes either private key and prevents double-rotation via on-chain state invalidation. The proposal's core claim — 'ZKP is the only mechanism that proves simultaneous possession of both keys without exposure' — is false. Standard ECDSA/EdDSA key delegation achieves identical guarantees. The ZKP adds proof generation latency (3–10s), circuit complexity, and a trusted setup ceremony for zero marginal security or privacy benefit.

AXIS 1 CONCERN: The simpler path for Theseus is trivial — a Solidity (or custom VM) registry function that takes (new_pubkey, sig_by_old_key) and atomically invalidates the old key. A senior EVM dev completes this in 2–3 days. The ZKP circuit path (AgentRotation.circom + double-EdDSA witness + PLONK setup) is 2–4 weeks minimum, and the marginal security gain over the standard path is undemonstrated.

AXIS 3 CONCERN: The proposal conflates two distinct failure modes. For COMPROMISED keys (the headline use case), ZKP rotation is self-defeating: generating the rotation proof requires the old private key — an agent whose key is stolen cannot safely invoke this path without the attacker being able to generate a competing rotation proof first. The proposal solves scheduled/epoch-based rotation only, which standard crypto handles trivially.

AXIS 4 CONCERN: Theseus's VM compatibility is explicitly unresolved. If Theseus is non-EVM, the entire Solidity verifier layer is dead weight. The proposal is scoped as a 2-week sprint but requires: new Circom circuit, pot16.ptau constraint budget verification, PLONK ceremony, IdentityRegistry fork, and an unknown Theseus integration surface. Scope underestimation risk is high, and the value upside doesn't justify the integration surface given Axis 2's finding.

AXIS 5 PASS: The double-EdDSA witness pattern and nullifier-based rotation would generalize to other agent-native platforms (Fetch.ai, autonomous agent frameworks). Circuit artifacts would extend Bolyra's library. This is the proposal's only defensible dimension.

SCORING IMPACT: Axis 2 is a clean FAIL (standard crypto provably suffices). Axes 1, 3, 4 are CONCERNs. By rubric: one axis failure alone warrants demotion; three CONCERNs compound the weakness. The proposal survives only 1/5 axes cleanly — DROP threshold met.
tier3_summary: The ZKP key-rotation proposal's core premise is false — standard EdDSA delegation achieves identical security without exposing private keys, making the ZKP overhead unjustifiable; DROP.

title: Sybil-resistant agent reputation via ZKP nullifiers for agent marketplaces
description: Agent marketplaces collapse when reputation is cheap to fake — if spinning up a new agent costs nothing, a bad actor resets its reputation every time. Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit uses Semaphore v4-style nullifier hashing to prove 'this identity has not been seen before in this context' without revealing which identity it is. Adapting this to agent-rooted trust (where the Theseus genesis transaction replaces the human enrollment) gives each agent a unique, unlinkable nullifier that persists across sessions but cannot be duplicated. This is the foundational primitive for any reputation system on Theseus — Merit Systems' x402scan and Kite both build payment discovery on top of agent reputation, but neither provides Sybil resistance at the identity layer. World's agentkit attempts human-attestation as a proxy for uniqueness but explicitly excludes agent-native scenarios.

rationale.agent_need: The need is well-argued from structural constraints (no human key path, cheap agent spin-up) and confirmed by World agentkit's documented exclusion of agent-native scenarios, but direct Theseus documentation showing they've identified Sybil resistance as a gap is cited only from a context brief, not a primary source.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (stake-weighted reputation as in Fetch.ai) is named and its failure mode (stake can be cheaply recycled, no unlinkability) is structurally argued, though the analysis could more sharply address whether a simpler on-chain commitments scheme could achieve the same without a full ZKP circuit.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Specific circuits (HumanUniqueness fork, AgentPolicy, Delegation), contract functions, and realistic day-estimates are named with clear reuse rationale and no new trusted setup ceremony required, making this one of the better-specified proposals; minor risk is that the theseus-registry-adaptor and marketplace-admission-gate are new contracts requiring audit time not fully accounted for.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus lacks ZKP circuit expertise is plausible but not evidenced — no team composition data is cited, and Theseus could plausibly adopt Lit Protocol or commission a ZKP team, so the exclusivity of the leverage is moderate at best.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human-controlled key path, funded by Orange DAO; identity approach is likely raw EdDSA keypairs with no Sybil-resistance layer documented.
x402scan (Merit Systems) and Kite build payment discovery on agent reputation but explicitly lack a Sybil-resistance primitive at the identity layer, confirmed by the opportunity description.
Lit Protocol provides programmable key management and access control for agents but relies on threshold MPC rather than ZKP nullifiers, offering no unlinkable uniqueness guarantee.
Turnkey offers agent wallet infrastructure via secure enclaves but is key-custody focused with no reputation or Sybil-resistance primitive.
Privy handles embedded wallets and agent signing but is human-auth-first; agent-native Sybil resistance is out of scope.
World's agentkit uses human World ID attestation as a proxy for agent uniqueness but explicitly excludes agent-native scenarios, leaving a documented gap Bolyra can fill.
Fetch.ai's Agentverse assigns DIDs to agents but reputation is stake-weighted, not Sybil-resistant at cryptographic level; no ZKP nullifier mechanism present.
SingularityNET uses on-chain agent registry with AGIX staking for reputation; no nullifier-based uniqueness, easy to duplicate with fresh stake.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + DIF DIDComm v2 (standards)
evidence.summary: DID specs support agent DIDs but provide no uniqueness or Sybil-resistance guarantees; nullifier extension would be non-standard and novel.
Emerging IETF AI agent auth draft focuses on OAuth delegation scopes, not cryptographic uniqueness; ZKP nullifier layer would be additive.

Semaphore v4 is the production-grade ZKP nullifier primitive; Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit already reuses its ceremony, making adaptation to agent-rooted trust a circuit-level extension rather than a new trusted setup.
Aztec uses ZKP for account abstraction but not for cross-context Sybil resistance; nullifier model differs from Semaphore's context-scoped design.
Polygon ID deploys ZKP credentials on EVM chains but is credential-presentation focused, not uniqueness/nullifier focused; no agent-native reputation primitive.

mapped_primitives.usage: The Poseidon2(scope, secret) nullifier is the direct Sybil-resistance primitive. For agents, the 'secret' is replaced by a deterministic value derived from the Theseus genesis transaction hash (e.g., Poseidon2(genesisTxHash, operatorSalt)). The scope parameterization means the same agent gets an unlinkable nullifier per marketplace, so reputation is siloed by context. Merkle membership proof (LeanIMT depth 20) handles ~1M registered agents without a new ceremony — reuses the existing agentTree in IdentityRegistry rather than humanTree.

Selective disclosure of the permission bitmask maps directly to reputation tiers — an agent proves 'my reputation tier satisfies requiredScopeMask bits' without revealing the full credential. The identity-bound scope commitment Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp) can encode a reputation score or tier as the bitmask, with expiry enforcing time-decaying reputation. EdDSA operator signature means a trusted reputation oracle (Theseus validator set or Merit Systems) signs the credential off-chain, and the circuit proves it without on-chain oracle calls.

When a marketplace agent spawns task sub-agents, the delegation chain enforces that the sub-agent's reputation scope is a bitwise subset of the parent's — a low-reputation parent cannot spawn a high-reputation sub-agent. The 3-hop limit and nullifier replay protection on delegation prevent reputation laundering through chain inflation. delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry ensures reputation leases don't outlive the parent credential.

The existing agentTree (LeanIMT with 30-entry root history buffer) is the Merkle accumulator for registered Theseus agents. Atomic handshake verification proves both uniqueness (Groth16 nullifier proof) and reputation level (PLONK AgentPolicy proof) in a single transaction, which is the correct surface for marketplace admission gates. Delegation nullifier replay protection directly prevents the reset-reputation attack described in the opportunity.

The x402 payment integration (integrations/payment-protocols/) provides the monetization rail that reputation gates. An agent with a proven nullifier and reputation tier can unlock x402 paywalls on Theseus-connected services (x402scan, Kite discovery) without deanonymizing itself. The existing integration surface only needs a Bolyra proof check inserted before the payment authorization step.

new_components_needed.description: AgentGenesis circuit: takes Theseus genesis tx hash as the enrollment anchor (replacing human secret in HumanUniqueness). Computes agent identity commitment as Poseidon2(genesisTxHash, operatorSalt) and produces a nullifier Poseidon2(marketplaceScope, genesisCommitment). Needs a storage proof or Merkle inclusion proof that the genesis tx exists on Theseus chain — the non-trivial part is the cross-chain witness. Can fork HumanUniqueness (~16K constraints) with the secret input replaced; storage proof adds ~4-8K constraints depending on trie depth.

TheseusReputationRegistry: deployed on Theseus (or Base Sepolia with Theseus bridge). Stores nullifier→reputationCommitment mappings. Exposes admitAgent(nullifierHash, reputationProof) and revokeAgent(nullifierHash) guarded by the Groth16/PLONK verifiers already deployed. Root history buffer pattern from IdentityRegistry copy-pastes directly. Includes a reputation update path: operator signs new credential, agent proves continuity via nullifier binding without re-enrollment.

Theseus SDK bridge (@bolyra/theseus): thin adapter mapping Theseus chain's agent API to @bolyra/sdk createAgentCredential / proveHandshake. Mirrors the @bolyra/openclaw pattern. Handles genesis tx extraction from Theseus RPC, operatorSalt derivation, and proof submission to TheseusReputationRegistry. Also wraps x402 payment headers with Bolyra proof attestations so x402scan can filter by proven reputation tier.

Reputation claim extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 and did:bolyra method: defines reputationTier claim type, nullifier binding to genesis tx, and scope encoding for marketplace contexts. Required for Merit Systems / Kite interop so they can verify proofs without coupling to Bolyra internals. Adds ~5 test vectors to the existing conformance suite.

theseus_integration_surface: Four connection points: (1) Verifier contracts — deploy PlonkVerifier and Groth16Verifier (already live on Base Sepolia) to Theseus EVM or bridge via a light-client relay; TheseusReputationRegistry calls into them on-chain, same pattern as IdentityRegistry. (2) SDK bridge — @bolyra/theseus npm package wraps Theseus RPC calls (genesis tx extraction, agentTree leaf construction) and feeds @bolyra/sdk proveHandshake; marketplace operators call one function to get a proof bundle. (3) x402 middleware — insert Bolyra proof verification as an x402 payment authorization hook; x402scan and Kite read the proof attestation from the HTTP 402 challenge response headers rather than building their own Sybil layer. (4) DID resolution — Theseus agents register did:bolyra DIDs that resolve to their agentTree leaf; marketplace UIs resolve DIDs to reputation commitments without touching nullifiers, preserving unlinkability at the presentation layer.

deliverables.name: agent-nullifier-circuit
deliverables.description: Minor fork of HumanUniqueness.circom: replace human secret leaf with Poseidon2(genesisTxHash, operatorSalt), keep LeanIMT depth-20 membership proof and scope-parameterized nullifier hash intact. Reuses pot16.ptau and agentTree in IdentityRegistry — no new trusted setup ceremony needed. Change is ~15 lines of Circom.

deliverables.name: reputation-tier-spec
deliverables.description: Defines how AgentPolicy 8-bit bitmask encodes reputation tiers (bit 0=enrolled, bit 1=verified-payment, bit 2=trusted-small-tx, bit 3=trusted-medium-tx, bits 4-7 reserved for Theseus-native tiers). Specifies EdDSA credential issuance flow: Theseus validator set OR Bolyra-operated oracle signs off-chain, circuit proves tier ≥ threshold without revealing score. Defines expiry convention for time-decaying reputation leases.

deliverables.name: theseus-registry-adaptor
deliverables.description: Thin Solidity contract wrapping IdentityRegistry: accepts (genesisTxHash, operatorSalt, agentLeaf, merkleProof) and inserts agent leaf into agentTree. Enforces one-genesis-one-leaf via a mapping(bytes32 => bool) of spent genesis hashes. Emits AgentEnrolled(nullifierHash, scope). Deploys alongside existing IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia.

deliverables.name: marketplace-admission-gate
deliverables.description: Solidity contract verifying a (AgentNullifier Groth16 proof + AgentPolicy PLONK proof) pair atomically in a single transaction. Tracks spent nullifiers per scope to block reputation-reset attacks. Exposes admitAgent(nullifierProof, reputationProof, requiredTierMask) → bool. Designed as a composable primitive Theseus marketplace contracts can call as a modifier.

deliverables.name: sdk-theseus-adapter
deliverables.description: TS SDK extension exported from @bolyra/sdk v0.3: theseusGenesisToCredential(genesisTxHash, operatorPrivKey, reputationTier, expiry) → AgentCredential (wraps createAgentCredential with genesis-derived secret). proveTheseusAdmission(credential, scope, requiredMask) calls proveHandshake() against agentTree root. Includes TypeScript types for TheseusGenesisCredential.

deliverables.name: x402-reputation-gate
deliverables.description: Wire Bolyra proof check into existing x402 payment-protocols integration: before x402 authorization executes, require valid (AgentNullifier + minimum reputation tier) proof. Agents below threshold receive HTTP 402 with a Bolyra enrollment URL in WWW-Authenticate header. Connects to x402scan and Kite discovery via their existing payment middleware hooks.

deliverables.name: theseus-sybil-demo
deliverables.description: Single Node script showing the full attack-and-defense loop on Base Sepolia: (1) enroll agent keyed to a real Theseus genesis tx hash, (2) issue reputation credential at tier 2 via oracle, (3) prove admission to mock marketplace gate contract, (4) attempt second enrollment with same genesis hash → reverted. Console output shows nullifier hash, proof time, and block explorer link. Runs in under 2 minutes end-to-end.

deliverables.name: theseus-integration-spec
deliverables.description: Technical doc for Theseus team covering: genesis tx hash derivation convention and salt spec, contract ABI and deployment addresses on Base Sepolia, proof flow diagram (enroll → oracle-sign → prove → gate), oracle key custody options (Theseus validator multisig vs Bolyra-operated), EVM compatibility checklist, and LeanIMT depth-20 capacity analysis (~1M agents).

spec.demo_description: For the Sunday call (tomorrow): show the existing AgentPolicy circuit live — prove a reputation tier from an EdDSA-signed credential in ~50ms via snarkjs, no server needed. Then open HumanUniqueness.circom and show the 15-line diff that swaps the human secret for Poseidon2(genesisTxHash, operatorSalt) — making the circuit change feel trivially concrete. Walk through the integration spec doc showing the full proof flow diagram. Key talking point: the Sybil-resistance primitive already works, the Theseus integration is a 2-week engineering task not a research problem. Ask Theseus for genesis tx format spec and EVM compatibility confirmation to unblock day-1 work.
risks: Theseus is not EVM-compatible: Solidity verifier contracts cannot deploy; would require porting Groth16/PLONK verifiers to their VM or moving verification off-chain with on-chain commitment anchoring — adds 2-3 weeks.

Genesis tx hash is not stable pre-finality or not accessible to the agent proving environment: need a trusted relay mechanism, which reintroduces a centralization point the ZKP was supposed to eliminate.

Reputation oracle key custody unresolved: EdDSA signing key needs a clear owner (Theseus validator set, Bolyra-operated oracle, or multi-sig) before production; wrong choice here becomes the system's trust bottleneck.

agentTree root history buffer (30 entries) saturates under rapid Theseus agent registration bursts, causing membership proof failures for agents enrolled before the buffer rolled over — may need to extend buffer or add an archival root checkpoint.
World agentkit or Lit Protocol ship a competing nullifier primitive in the next 90 days given the documented gap; speed of demo to Theseus matters more than polish.
spec.success_criteria: A single Base Sepolia transaction: registers a Theseus agent keyed to a real genesis tx hash, verifies a Groth16 nullifier proof that no prior enrollment exists for that hash in that marketplace scope, verifies a PLONK AgentPolicy proof that reputation tier >= required threshold, and rejects a second registration attempt with the same genesis hash — all with end-to-end proving time under 500ms on commodity hardware and gas under 300k.
spec.theseus_dependencies: EVM compatibility confirmation or custom VM ABI if not EVM; genesis transaction hash derivation spec (algorithm, salt convention, finality timing); testnet RPC endpoint and faucet (Base Sepolia or Theseus devnet); decision on whether Theseus validator set will operate the EdDSA reputation oracle or Bolyra runs one independently; approximate target agent count at launch to validate LeanIMT depth-20 capacity of ~1M agents.

tier3_findings: AXIS 2 FAIL: The ZKP unlinkability property directly conflicts with the reputation use case. Reputation systems require persistent, linkable identity within a marketplace context. The card's own design (same nullifier per agent per marketplace) means the agent IS identifiable within any given marketplace — ZKP only hides cross-marketplace correlation. But Theseus agent genesis transactions are already public on-chain, so 'which agent you are' is not hidden data. A standard on-chain nullifier — keccak256(genesisTxHash, marketplaceId, salt) stored in a mapping — provides identical Sybil resistance with zero ZKP overhead, no trusted setup, and trivial verification. The kill signal fires: the ZKP privacy property conceals data that has no economic or security value because it is already public.

AXIS 1 CONCERN: The proposal describes the required circuit modification as 'minor' — replacing the human secret with a genesis tx hash. This self-assessment undermines the moat argument. Semaphore v4 is open source, PSE provides direct developer support, and a single ZKP-curious engineer can adapt the protocol in 4-6 weeks. Theseus is Orange DAO-backed ($100K), typical of YC-adjacent founding teams with high technical depth. The kill signal for 2-week replication in pure Solidity does not fire, but the Semaphore fork path makes 6-week replication realistic — well within the 3-month threshold.

AXIS 3 CONCERN: Theseus is pre-mainnet, pre-marketplace, and funded at $100K. Agent population at launch will be in the tens to low hundreds, where reputation is handled manually or by operator whitelist. Sybil resistance at cryptographic level only becomes necessary when anonymous participation is possible and reputation can be gamed at scale — typically >1K agents with open admission. The named use case (agent marketplace reputation) is valid but premature; Theseus needs a functioning L1 and marketplace first, making this a 12-18 month need, not 3-month.

AXIS 4 CONCERN: Theseus is a custom L1 with no confirmed EVM compatibility. The card assumes a Solidity verifier can be deployed, but if Theseus runs a custom VM (likely given the 'no human key path' architectural constraint that makes standard EVM account models awkward), Bolyra would need to implement a native Groth16 verifier in the Theseus VM language — a multi-month effort requiring cryptographic engineering beyond circuit work. The theseus-registry-adaptor and marketplace-admission-gate contracts are unaudited new surface. Gas/compute cost of on-chain Groth16 verification (~250K-500K EVM gas equivalent) on a nascent L1 with unknown fee markets is unquantified.

AXIS 5 PASS: The AgentUniqueness circuit (HumanUniqueness adapted for genesis-tx enrollment) is genuinely reusable. Context-scoped nullifiers for agent Sybil resistance is a primitive applicable to Fetch.ai, SingularityNET, and any future agent L1 or marketplace. This work advances Bolyra's agent-rooted trust roadmap independent of Theseus's survival. The reuse case is real and the primitives are not Theseus-specific.
tier3_summary: Standard on-chain nullifiers (keccak256 commitments) solve the Sybil problem with zero ZKP overhead, the circuit modification is self-described as minor making the Semaphore fork path trivially available to Theseus, and the target company is pre-mainnet with a $100K seed — three compounding weaknesses that make this a DROP under the rubric's two-or-fewer-axes threshold.

title: ZKP Agent Birth Certificate via PLONK (No Per-Agent Trusted Setup)
description: When a new agent spawns on Theseus, it needs a verifiable identity commitment that (a) doesn't require a human ceremony, (b) proves the agent was legitimately born from the L1 (not fabricated off-chain), and (c) is portable to other chains. Bolyra's PLONK-based AgentPolicy circuit avoids per-agent trusted setup — the universal SRS is the ceremony. An agent can generate its credential commitment at spawn time, submit a PLONK proof to the IdentityRegistry, and receive a DID (did:bolyra:...) anchored to the Theseus genesis block. This directly addresses the 'no human key path' constraint: the L1 itself is the root of trust, and ZKP is what proves legitimate birth without a human CA. Bolyra already ships PLONK zkeys for AgentPolicy — this is a configuration extension, not a new circuit.

rationale.agent_need: Theseus documentation is not publicly accessible so evidence rests on structural inference from 'no human key path' plus weak analogies from Fetch.ai/SingularityNET that use conventional PKI, not ZKP birth certs.
rationale.zkp_edge: The proposal names Lit Protocol (MPC-based) and Turnkey (TEE-based) as non-ZKP alternatives and correctly identifies that neither produces portable, chain-anchored ZKP credential commitments, but the failure mode argument is asserted rather than demonstrated with a concrete attack or limitation scenario.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Existing PLONK zkeys, PlonkVerifier, IdentityRegistry, and createAgentCredential() are all directly reusable; new work is a thin SDK wrapper (~0.5d), one orchestration contract (~1d), a spec addendum (~0.5d), and a demo — well-scoped and realistic.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build this in <3 months is plausible given Circom/PLONK expertise requirements, but the proposal doesn't assess whether Theseus has a crypto team, whether they are already evaluating Lit/Turnkey, or why they would prefer Bolyra over a custodial TEE solution that ships faster.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (not reachable via search in this session)
evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 founded 2025, funded by Orange DAO; no public documentation of their identity/auth primitive was accessible — likely raw EdDSA keypairs at spawn time with no ZKP layer.

Lit Protocol offers programmable key management via threshold MPC for agents and dApps, but relies on a human-operated node network and does NOT produce ZKP-verifiable credential commitments — no PLONK or Groth16 proofs anchored to chain genesis.

Turnkey provides TEE-backed key custody for agents (used by Coinbase Agentic Wallets per signal URL), but custody is custodial/TEE-attested rather than ZKP-proven, and does not produce portable DID documents or on-chain credential registries.
Privy targets human-facing embedded wallets, has begun supporting server-side agent wallets, but assumes a human operator root of trust — not suited for Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core Spec / DIF DIDComm v2 / IETF draft-klrc-aiagent
evidence.summary: No ratified standard for agent-to-agent ZKP-backed DID authentication exists as of mid-2026; DIF and IETF working groups have drafts but none address agent-native L1 genesis anchoring or PLONK-based birth certificates.

Fetch.ai agents carry on-chain identities (agent addresses) but use conventional PKI signing — no ZKP birth certificate, no universal SRS, and delegation is handled via smart contract logic rather than circuit-enforced scope narrowing.
SingularityNET's agent registry is metadata-only (IPFS CIDs + on-chain hashes); no cryptographic proof-of-legitimate-spawn and no delegation circuit.

evidence.source: AWS blog: x402 and Agentic Commerce (signal URL)
evidence.summary: x402 HTTP payment protocol for agents relies on wallet-signed requests — highlights demand for agent-native credential primitives but does not provide identity birth certificates or ZKP proofs.

Coinbase's agentic wallet product uses Turnkey TEE custody — confirms strong market demand for agent key management at spawn time but remains TEE/MPC rather than ZKP, leaving the 'portable, no-ceremony, L1-anchored proof' gap open.

zkLogin proves OAuth identity via Groth16 on Sui — closest deployed ZKP identity primitive, but human-rooted (OAuth) and chain-specific; does not generalize to agent-spawn proofs on arbitrary EVM-compatible L1s.

Semaphore provides ZKP group membership with nullifiers (Bolyra reuses its ceremony for HumanUniqueness), but is explicitly human-biometric-rooted — cannot serve as agent birth certificate without stripping the biometric assumption.

evidence.source: Bolyra CLAUDE.md / AgentPolicy PLONK zkeys (local context)
evidence.summary: Bolyra already ships PLONK zkeys for AgentPolicy with universal SRS (pot16.ptau), 8-bit cumulative permission encoding, and a DID method (did:bolyra:) — the primitives for an agent birth certificate without per-agent ceremony are already built.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core birth-certificate primitive. Agent generates credentialCommitment at spawn time using createAgentCredential(). PLONK universal SRS IS the ceremony — no per-agent trusted setup. The operatorKey role is filled by Theseus L1's own signing authority (sequencer/validator key or a deterministic key derived from the genesis block hash), making L1 the root of trust without a human CA. Selective disclosure of permission bitmask lets the agent prove capability subset to any relying party without revealing full policy.

agentTree (LeanIMT, depth 20) registers the agent's leaf after PLONK proof verification. Root history buffer (30 entries) provides stale-proof tolerance during chain reorgs. Nonce equality and delegation nullifier replay protection guard against duplicate registrations across forks. For Theseus-native deployment the contract needs redeployment on Theseus's EVM (same bytecode, new address); Base Sepolia can serve as the canonical registry with a cross-chain relay if Theseus is EVM-compatible.

Already deployed at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db on Base Sepolia. The same vkey.json can generate a verifier contract deployable on Theseus's chain with zero circuit changes. Self-contained PLONK proof portability means the proof bundle (pi_a, pi_b, pi_c + public inputs) can be verified by any chain holding the verification key — cross-chain portability is inherent.

Enables Theseus sub-agent spawning with enforced scope narrowing. A parent agent's birth certificate becomes the delegator credential; child agents receive scope-narrowed delegatee credentials with delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry. Three-hop max (human -> orchestrator agent -> task agent -> sub-agent) maps cleanly to Theseus's agent hierarchy. Delegator key binding (UC3.1 fix) ensures the birth certificate chain is unforgeable.

DID anchored to Theseus genesis block: did:bolyra:agent:<agentId> where agentId = Poseidon(genesisBlockHash, credentialCommitment). The DID document carries the PLONK proof reference, agentTree Merkle path, and chain-of-origin metadata. Portable resolution — any resolver with the verification key can validate without contacting Theseus's chain directly. Minor extension to the DID method spec to include genesisAnchor as a first-class DID document field.

createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorKey, permissions, expiry) is the direct entry point. modelHash encodes the agent's code digest; operatorKey is Theseus L1's signing key (or a key derived from genesis hash via a deterministic KDF). No new SDK function needed — a genesisAnchor option parameter added to the options object satisfies the L1-birth provenance requirement without breaking the existing API.

The mutual ZKP auth handshake (proveHandshake + verifyHandshake) becomes the Theseus agent-to-agent trust handshake. When two Theseus agents interact, both present birth-certificate-backed proofs; the IETF draft's nonce binding prevents replay across sessions. This gives Theseus a standards-track wire format instead of a custom auth protocol.

new_components_needed.description: genesis-anchor convention: define how Theseus genesis block hash is incorporated into credentialCommitment — specifically, a Poseidon3(modelHash, genesisBlockHash, operatorPubKeyHash) preimage convention so L1 provenance is cryptographically bound. Extend did:bolyra method spec to add genesisAnchor as a first-class field in the DID document and add 3–5 test vectors to test-vectors.json.

Add optional genesisAnchor parameter to createAgentCredential options and incorporate it into the credential commitment hash. Expose a deriveOperatorKeyFromGenesis(genesisBlockHash) helper using Baby Jubjub deterministic scalar derivation so Theseus L1 can act as operator without a human-generated key. Backwards-compatible — existing callers unaffected.

theseus-bolyra-spawn adapter: a lightweight TS module that hooks into Theseus's agent spawn lifecycle, calls createAgentCredential at birth, submits the PLONK proof to IdentityRegistry (or Theseus-native verifier), and returns a did:bolyra DID to the agent runtime. Pattern matches langchain-bolyra-tool stub already in integrations/.

Deploy PlonkVerifier + IdentityRegistry on Theseus's EVM (if EVM-compatible). No Solidity changes needed — same bytecode, new deployment script targeting Theseus RPC. If Theseus is not EVM-compatible, a thin verification relay contract on Base Sepolia with cross-chain message passing (e.g. via L1 state proof) is the fallback.

theseus_integration_surface: Three connection points: (1) SDK bridge — Theseus agent runtime imports @bolyra/sdk, calls createAgentCredential() at spawn with genesis-anchored operatorKey, receives a PLONK proof bundle and did:bolyra DID; (2) Verifier contract — PlonkVerifier + IdentityRegistry deployed on Theseus's EVM chain (or Base Sepolia as canonical registry with Theseus relaying proofs via L1 state root); (3) DID resolution — Theseus's agent directory resolves did:bolyra DIDs against the agentTree Merkle root, enabling any other Theseus agent or external service to verify birth provenance without trusting Theseus's own assertions. The mutual ZKP handshake (draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01) then serves as the agent-to-agent trust wire protocol, giving Theseus a standards-track inter-agent auth layer on top of the birth certificate foundation.

End-to-end runtime under 5 seconds.

Etherscan link showing AgentRegistered event on Base Sepolia with credentialCommitment and genesisAnchor visible. One comparison slide: Lit Protocol = MPC custody (node network, no ZKP), Turnkey = TEE attestation (custodial, no portable DID), Privy = human root required. Bolyra = ZKP-verifiable birth cert, no human CA, PLONK universal SRS IS the ceremony, portable to any EVM chain. Closing line: 'The Theseus genesis block replaces the human ceremony. The L1 is the trust root.'
risks: Theseus is not EVM-compatible: TheseusBirthCertRegistrar cannot deploy natively — mitigate by positioning Base Sepolia as canonical registry with a cross-chain relay stub; proof portability is inherent (any chain holding vkey.json can verify)

Solo founder bandwidth: 2.5 days of work must land by Saturday EOD — if time-pressed, cut contract deploy and show local proof verification only; the cryptography story holds without the on-chain tx
spec.success_criteria: Demo runs end-to-end without errors from a cold clone: agent seed + genesis hash in, PLONK proof verified, DID printed, AgentRegistered event visible on Base Sepolia Etherscan. The integration is working when Eric Wang asks 'how do we wire this into our agent spawn flow on mainnet?' — that question is the buying signal.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Nice-to-have before Sunday, none are blockers: (1) Theseus testnet RPC endpoint to pull real genesis block hash — mock substitutes cleanly. (2) Confirmation of EVM compatibility for native TheseusBirthCertRegistrar deploy — Base Sepolia serves as canonical registry in the interim. (3) Their current agent spawn API/spec so operatorKey derivation path matches their actual key generation — determines whether Poseidon or keccak256 derivation is the right join point. All three can be collected on the Sunday call and wired in as a follow-up PR.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 (Theseus Builds It) — CONCERN: The core 'proof of legitimate spawn' can be replicated without ZKP expertise in ~2 weeks. Theseus L1 can deterministically derive an agent keypair from the genesis transaction, register the public key in a Solidity registry, and use the spawning transaction hash as the birth certificate. That's standard OpenZeppelin + Hardhat work. The proposal conflates 'no human CA' with 'needs ZKP' — these are not the same property. Bolyra's actual moat is selective disclosure of the 8-bit permission bitmask, not the birth certificate itself.

AXIS 2 (Standard Crypto Suffices) — FAIL: This is the kill signal. The specific ZKP value-add named in the proposal is (a) no per-agent trusted setup and (b) portable credential commitment. But the 'no ceremony' argument only matters if you're using ZKP at all — it's circular. Standard crypto provides proof of legitimate spawn via the L1's own signature on the genesis transaction. For cross-chain portability, Theseus can publish a Merkle root of its agent registry, and any chain can verify inclusion proofs without ZKP. The only genuine ZKP advantage is hiding the full permission bitmask, but at birth-certificate time there is no adversary who benefits from not knowing an agent's permission scope — permissions are typically set by the operator and are non-sensitive at spawn.

AXIS 3 (No Agent Actually Needs This) — CONCERN: No concrete Theseus agent use case is named that fails without ZKP birth certs. The proposal explicitly admits 'evidence rests on structural inference.' The need for permission-private birth certificates only becomes real when (a) an agent has sensitive permission scope it wants to selectively disclose to counterparties, and (b) those counterparties exist and are querying the agent. At Theseus's current scale ($100K funding, pre-mainnet), neither condition holds. The earliest plausible concrete need is agent-to-agent delegation with permission narrowing — but that's Axis 4 work, not birth certificate work.

AXIS 4 (Integration Complexity vs Value) — FAIL: Two blockers. First, Theseus's VM is unknown. The proposal assumes EVM-compatible PlonkVerifier.sol deployment, but a custom L1 (which Theseus may well be building) requires implementing a PLONK verifier from scratch or porting the Solidity verifier to their native execution environment — multi-month work. Second, the proving side assumes agents can invoke snarkjs or rapidsnark at spawn time. Agents spawning on-chain cannot run JavaScript natively; they would need to call out to a Bolyra-hosted proving service or run the prover in a sidecar. This is a trusted off-chain dependency that undermines the 'no human key path' constraint Theseus is explicitly designed to avoid. Neither blocker is addressed in the proposal.

AXIS 5 (Single-Partner Dependency) — PASS: The new work is genuinely minimal (~2d) and entirely within existing Bolyra primitives (PLONK zkeys, AgentPolicy circuit, DID method). If Theseus fails, the orchestration contract and SDK wrapper are reusable for any other agent-native chain. This axis survives cleanly.

DEMOTION MATH: FAIL on axes 2 and 4, CONCERN on axes 1 and 3, PASS on axis 5. Per rubric, 2 FAILs = survives fewer than 3/5 = DROP regardless of raw score. Original verdict was CONSIDER; adversarial review mandates REJECT.
tier3_summary: The ZKP birth certificate collapses under Axis 2 (a spawning transaction signature IS the proof of legitimate birth, and permission bitmask privacy is not valuable at spawn time) and Axis 4 (unknown Theseus VM + on-chain agents cannot run snarkjs without a trusted off-chain proving service that contradicts the 'no human key path' constraint).

title: Nullifier-Bound Session Nonces to Block Agent Transaction Replay Attacks
description: Agents on Theseus execute high-frequency autonomous transactions — authorization proofs issued at time T can be replayed at T+N if the receiving contract or counterparty agent does not enforce per-session uniqueness. Unlike human systems where replay surfaces are limited by human interaction speed, agents can replay thousands of stale authorizations per second. Bolyra's handshake protocol already binds every proof to a fresh sessionNonce and enforces nullifier uniqueness on-chain — replaying (humanProof, agentProof) without rebinding the nonce fails verification by design. Porting this nullifier registry to Theseus L1 closes a structural replay surface that raw EdDSA signatures cannot address (a valid signature is valid forever unless explicitly revoked).

rationale.agent_need: High-frequency autonomous agent transactions create a real replay surface absent from human systems, and x402 agentic commerce evidence confirms authorization replay is a live concern, though direct Theseus documentation showing they lack nullifier protection is inferred rather than cited.
rationale.zkp_edge: The structural argument that EdDSA signatures are valid forever without explicit revocation is correct, but nonce counters at the wallet layer (Privy pattern) or simple on-chain nonce mappings achieve replay prevention without ZKP — the ZKP advantage here is unlinkable nullifiers for privacy, not replay prevention per se, which weakens the edge claim.
rationale.primitive_readiness: IdentityRegistry, AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, and Delegation circuits all map directly with no or minor modifications, the deploy script is estimated at 0.5 days reusing existing artifacts, and the SDK chain config is a one-file edit — highest confidence dimension with concrete named primitives and realistic timelines.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The expertise gap argument (Circom proficiency, trusted setup) is plausible but unverified against Theseus's team composition, and competitors Lit Protocol and Turnkey could offer simpler signature-based alternatives that sidestep ZKP entirely, making the partnership value proposition dependent on privacy requirements Theseus may not have.

evidence.summary: Theseus is a $100K Orange DAO-backed L1 purpose-built for autonomous AI agents with no human-controlled key path, almost certainly relying on raw EdDSA keypairs with no native nullifier/replay protection.
AWS x402 agentic commerce blog highlights autonomous agent payment flows where replay of authorization headers is a live attack surface — no nullifier mechanism is present in x402 by default.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol — programmable key pairs (PKP) for agent signing
evidence.summary: Lit PKPs provide threshold-ECDSA agent keys and access control conditions but offer no ZKP nullifier registry; valid signatures remain valid forever unless Lit node network explicitly revokes.

evidence.source: Turnkey — secure MPC key management for wallets/agents
evidence.summary: Turnkey manages EdDSA/ECDSA agent keys via MPC enclaves; authorization tokens are signature-bound but not nullifier-bound, so replayed tokens within TTL windows are indistinguishable from fresh ones.

evidence.source: Privy — embedded wallets and agent signers
evidence.summary: Privy focuses on human-facing embedded wallets; agent signer capability is nascent and has no replay-prevention primitive beyond standard nonce counters at the wallet layer.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 — ZKP identity with nullifier registry (Ethereum)
evidence.summary: Semaphore's on-chain nullifier registry is the closest deployed analog to what Bolyra proposes; Bolyra already reuses its Groth16 ceremony, making a Theseus port directly feasible without new trusted setup.

evidence.source: Polygon ID — ZKP credentials on EVM chains
evidence.summary: Polygon ID uses ZKP-based credential proofs on EVM but is human-identity-centric with no agent-native session nonce binding or high-frequency replay prevention design.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai / Autonolas — agent-native L1 identity patterns
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai and Autonolas use agent DIDs with basic signature verification; neither implements nullifier-based per-session uniqueness, leaving replay surfaces open in high-frequency multi-agent coordination.

evidence.source: IETF draft-ietf-oauth-transaction-tokens + agent auth working groups
evidence.summary: Transaction tokens proposal introduces per-request binding but is OAuth/HTTP layer — no cryptographic nullifier commitment; does not prevent replay at the smart contract or L1 execution layer.

evidence.source: W3C Decentralized Identifiers (DID) spec — agent DID methods
evidence.summary: DID spec has no provisions for session nonce binding or nullifier uniqueness; agent DIDs inherit the same 'valid signature = valid forever' weakness as raw EdDSA.

evidence.source: EIP-2612 permit / account abstraction (EIP-4337) nonce handling
evidence.summary: EVM account abstraction handles sequential nonces for transaction ordering but not ZKP-bound per-session nullifiers; an agent with a compromised replay window can exhaust multi-call permits.

evidence.source: Bolyra CLAUDE.md — handshake nonce binding pattern
evidence.summary: Bolyra's sessionNonce binding is already implemented and circuit-enforced; porting the nullifier registry contract to an EVM-compatible Theseus chain is days of work, not months — primitive_readiness score of 22/25 is credible.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core replay-prevention infrastructure already exists: nonce equality enforcement prevents (humanProof, agentProof) reuse, delegation nullifier replay protection blocks stale delegation chains, root history buffers (30 entries) accommodate Theseus block latency. This contract is the direct analog of the 'nullifier registry' the opportunity calls for. Deploying it to Theseus L1 is the primary integration act.

Expiry enforcement (expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp) and identity-bound scope commitment directly prevent authorization proofs issued at T from being valid at T+N beyond their stated window. The selective-disclosure bitmask means counterparty agents learn only required permissions, not the full credential — reducing the value of replayed proofs even if nullifier check were bypassed.

Session nonce binding is a first-class output: the circuit commits to sessionNonce as a public signal, so any replayed (humanProof, nonce) pair with a stale nonce fails on-chain without requiring application-level state. Poseidon2(scope, secret) nullifier is unlinkable across scopes, preventing cross-session correlation attacks specific to high-frequency agent environments.

Delegation nullifier replay protection (already in IdentityRegistry) closes the sub-agent replay surface: a delegated authorization cannot be replayed after use or after the delegator revokes. Expiry narrowing (delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry) bounds the replay window for the entire chain, not just the leaf credential. Current-time liveness check enforces this at proof-time.
Needs to be deployed to Theseus L1 as a standalone contract. Verifies HumanUniqueness proofs on-chain — the cryptographic anchor for the nullifier check.
Needs to be deployed to Theseus L1. Verifies AgentPolicy and Delegation proofs. PLONK's universal SRS means no Theseus-specific trusted setup ceremony is required.
Needs to be deployed to Theseus L1 alongside PlonkVerifier for delegation chain verification with hop-count enforcement.

proveHandshake already generates a fresh sessionNonce and binds both proofs to it. verifyHandshake already enforces nonce equality. The only change needed is wiring the Theseus L1 chain ID and RPC endpoint into the SDK's chain config so it submits to the right IdentityRegistry deployment.

new_components_needed.description: Deploy IdentityRegistry + Groth16Verifier + PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier to Theseus L1. Contracts are already written and audited on Base Sepolia — this is a deployment task, not a build task. Requires Theseus L1 RPC, funded deployer wallet, and verifying constructor args match the zkeys used in production.

Add Theseus L1 chain config to @bolyra/sdk (chain ID, RPC URL, IdentityRegistry address). Expose a theseusClient factory or extend the existing chain-config map. No circuit changes, no new proving logic.

Theseus agent adapter: thin wrapper that intercepts outbound agent authorization calls, calls proveHandshake with a fresh nonce, and submits the atomic handshake tx to the Theseus IdentityRegistry before executing the downstream action. Pattern mirrors the existing openclaw adapter. Required for the Sunday demo.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 FAIL — Replay prevention via nonce registries is trivially buildable without ZKP expertise. A usedNonces[bytes32] => bool mapping is 10 lines of Solidity and a well-understood OpenZeppelin pattern. EIP-4337 account abstraction handles sequential nonces natively. Theseus's 1-2 engineers can ship this in days, not months. ZKP expertise is not required for replay prevention — it is only required if the goal is *unlinkable* nullifiers, which is a privacy claim, not a security claim.

AXIS 2 FAIL — The card's own rationale self-destructs this proposal: 'nonce counters at the wallet layer or simple on-chain nonce mappings achieve replay prevention without ZKP — the ZKP advantage here is unlinkable nullifiers for privacy, not replay prevention per se.' That is an admission that standard crypto suffices for the stated goal. The ZKP overhead (Groth16 proof generation latency per session, ~200K-300K gas for on-chain verification vs ~20K gas for a boolean write) is not justified when the marginal benefit is privacy for session nonces — a property Theseus agents may not need on a transparent L1 where all transactions are visible anyway.

AXIS 3 CONCERN — The replay surface is real in high-frequency agent systems, but the ZKP-specific solution is not distinguishable from simpler alternatives at Theseus's current scale ($100K funded, pre-mainnet, likely single-digit active agents). The need for unlinkable nullifiers specifically requires an agent that (a) executes high-frequency transactions AND (b) needs to hide which sessions it participated in. No named Theseus agent use case satisfies both conditions. At launch scale, a simple nonce counter eliminates the replay surface entirely.

AXIS 4 CONCERN — Theseus's VM compatibility is an explicitly open question in the provided context. If Theseus runs a custom non-EVM VM, deploying Solidity verifier contracts requires either EVM compatibility or implementing a native Groth16 verifier — a significant engineering undertaking that could take months. Even on EVM, Groth16 verification at 200K-300K gas is prohibitively expensive for the 'thousands of transactions per second' agent pattern the proposal cites as its motivation. The integration complexity is therefore contingent on an unverified assumption (EVM compatibility) and economically suspect even if that assumption holds.

AXIS 5 PASS — Nullifier registry contracts and the underlying circuit artifacts (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy) are genuinely reusable across agent-native chains. A Theseus port would accelerate multi-chain deployment. This is the only axis the proposal survives cleanly.
SCORING SUMMARY — 1 clean PASS, 2 CONCERNs, 2 FAILs. Per rubric: 'Survives 2 or fewer: DROP regardless of raw score.' REJECT.
tier3_summary: The proposal's own rationale concedes that standard nonce mappings solve replay prevention without ZKP — the ZKP premium buys only unlinkable nullifiers (a privacy benefit, not a security one), and that benefit is unjustified for transparent L1 agent transactions at pre-mainnet scale where Theseus can ship the same functional protection in days with 15x lower gas cost.

title: ZKP delegation chain proof appended to x402 payment receipts for audit and dispute
description: Base's Builder Codes (ERC-8021) establish on-chain attribution for x402 payments, but attribution is not authorization — knowing which agent paid tells you nothing about whether it was permitted to pay, on whose behalf, or under what delegation chain. Bolyra can generate a compact ZKP that attaches to an x402 payment receipt and proves 'this payment was authorized by a valid, unrevoked delegation chain from root identity X with permission bit FINANCIAL_MEDIUM active at time T' without revealing X or the intermediate delegators. This is the audit and dispute primitive that enterprise operators and DeFi protocols will require before letting autonomous agents access high-value services. The x402 Foundation's Linux Foundation backing (Google, Visa, Stripe, AWS, Mastercard) signals regulatory scrutiny is coming; authorization proofs on payment receipts position Bolyra as the compliance layer for autonomous agent commerce.

rationale.agent_need: The attribution-vs-authorization distinction for x402 receipts is a structurally sound argument, and x402's Linux Foundation backing signals enterprise scrutiny is coming, but Theseus-specific evidence is absent (web search unavailable) so the claim that Theseus agents specifically need this remains inferred rather than cited.
rationale.zkp_edge: The ZKP edge is genuine and well-argued: a non-ZKP approach (EdDSA + on-chain ACL) either leaks the full delegation chain or requires O(n) on-chain lookups per hop, while the ZKP proves 'authorized by valid chain from root X with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM active at time T' without revealing X or intermediaries — a structural privacy and scalability win.
rationale.primitive_readiness: The Delegation + AgentPolicy circuits cover the core predicate, but the gap (no payment-context binding inputs) requires a major Delegation circuit modification, new zkeys from pot16.ptau, a regenerated Solidity verifier, and a new SDK export — realistically 10+ days of work with non-trivial ceremony overhead, which is correctly scoped but makes this a build rather than a near-term deploy.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The Circom/ZKP expertise gap is a real barrier for most chains, and the x402 compliance angle creates bilateral value, but the assessment that Theseus cannot build this in <3 months is speculative without confirming their team's crypto background, and the existence of MPC alternatives (Lit, Turnkey) that could partially satisfy enterprise audit requirements weakens the exclusivity argument.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human key path; agents hold their own keys and are first-class citizens, implying raw EdDSA keypairs with no delegation primitive as of the call date.
Could not retrieve Theseus GitHub repos, docs, or community discussions to confirm current identity/auth implementation details.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol (litprotocol.com) — known from training data

Turnkey offers sub-organization wallet scoping for agents but is custodial MPC, not ZKP-based, and has no delegation circuit with scope narrowing.
Privy targets human-rooted embedded wallets and has no agent-native delegation or ZKP authorization primitive.
Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication is active; no finalized spec for agent-to-agent delegation yet, leaving the standards gap Bolyra can anchor.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai / SingularityNET — known from training data
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai uses DID-based agent identifiers with on-chain registration but no ZKP delegation or cumulative permission encoding; SingularityNET relies on reputation oracles, not proofs.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 / Worldcoin — known from training data
evidence.summary: Semaphore v4 is the closest deployed ZKP identity primitive on Ethereum mainnet, but it is human-uniqueness only — no agent policy or delegation circuit exists in that stack.

evidence.source: x402 Foundation / Linux Foundation backing (provided context)
evidence.summary: x402 has Google, Visa, Stripe, AWS, Mastercard backing; regulatory scrutiny on agent payment authorization is imminent, creating pull for audit-grade delegation proofs on receipts.
ERC-8021 provides on-chain attribution (who paid) but explicitly not authorization (was the agent permitted to pay), confirming the gap Bolyra's delegation proof fills.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core cryptographic engine for the payment authorization proof. Already proves an unrevoked chain (human→agent→sub-agent, max 3 hops) with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM bit (bit 3, which implies bit 2 by cumulative encoding) active at a committed timestamp, without revealing the root identity or intermediate delegators. The identity-bound scope commitment chain and delegator key binding (UC3.1 fix) directly satisfy the 'authorized by valid delegation chain from root identity X' predicate. The liveness check (currentTimestamp < delegateeExpiry) anchors the proof to time T. Gap: circuit currently has no public input that commits to payment-specific parameters (amount, recipient address, tx/receipt hash), so a detached proof could be replayed against a different payment event.

Proves the paying agent holds FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3) with selective disclosure—only the required scope mask is revealed, not the full bitmask. The identity-bound scope commitment ties permission to the specific credential commitment, preventing credential substitution. Expiry enforcement and EdDSA operator signature verification are already in-circuit. This circuit runs in parallel with Delegation to prove both 'agent is credentialed' and 'agent is delegated' simultaneously, matching the handshake pattern.

Root history buffers (30 entries each for humanTree and agentTree) are the critical on-chain primitive here: a payment receipt must reference the Merkle root that was valid at payment time T, not the current root. The 30-entry buffer gives a sliding window to validate historical proofs. Delegation nullifier replay protection prevents the same authorization proof from being submitted twice for two different payments. The existing delegation chain-state tracking on-chain maps cleanly to audit trail requirements.

Already deployed at 0x285D2E315AdD9c82337d8a4BDF567AB3a9215B93 on Base Sepolia. x402 receipt verifiers can call this contract directly to check the PLONK proof attached to a payment receipt. PLONK's universal SRS means no per-payment trusted setup. This is the on-chain verification endpoint that x402 facilitators or Theseus infrastructure would invoke.

integrations/payment-protocols/src/spend-policy.ts already exists and implements budget/spend-policy enforcement logic. This is the SDK-level integration surface where payment authorization proofs get wired: SpendPolicy can be extended to call provePaymentAuthorization() before releasing an x402 HTTP 402 challenge response, attaching the Bolyra delegation proof to the receipt. The conformance runner structure there can host x402-specific test vectors.

new_components_needed.description: PaymentCommitmentBinding: extend Delegation.circom with a new public input `paymentCommitment = Poseidon4(paymentAmount, recipientAddress, receiptHash, paymentTimestamp)`. This binds the delegation proof to one specific payment event—a detached proof is cryptographically unusable on a different receipt. Implemented as a thin wrapper template around the existing Delegation circuit rather than a full rewrite. Requires new .zkey ceremony (pot16.ptau is sufficient at 22k constraints + ~2k new constraints).

X402AuthorizationVerifier: a Solidity contract that x402 facilitators and Theseus call. Accepts (receiptHash, delegationProof, agentProof, merkleRoot, permissionMask) and (1) calls DelegationPlonkVerifier.verifyProof(), (2) checks merkleRoot is in IdentityRegistry's root history buffer, (3) verifies paymentCommitment = Poseidon4(receiptHash params), (4) emits PaymentAuthorized(nullifier, scopeMask, expiryTimestamp) for the on-chain audit log. Wraps existing verifier contracts—no new cryptography.

provePaymentAuthorization(delegationChain, paymentParams): new SDK function that (1) resolves the active Merkle root at payment time from IdentityRegistry history, (2) computes paymentCommitment from payment params, (3) generates the extended Delegation PLONK proof with payment binding, (4) serializes to a compact base64url blob for x402 header attachment. Requires completing the delegate() v0.3 stub first—that's the primary dependency. Also needs a deserializePaymentProof() counterpart for verifiers.

X-Bolyra-Payment-Authorization HTTP header spec: defines the wire format for attaching a Bolyra proof to an x402 payment receipt. Fields: proof (base64url PLONK proof), publicSignals (paymentCommitment, nullifier, scopeMask, expiryTimestamp, merkleRoot), bolyraVersion. Extends draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with a new PaymentAuthorization proof type. Needed so x402 facilitators know what to parse and forward to X402AuthorizationVerifier.

ERC-8021 (Builder Codes) bridge: on-chain mapping from Bolyra nullifier to Base Builder Code attribution. When X402AuthorizationVerifier emits PaymentAuthorized, a bridge contract reads the Builder Code from the tx and records (builderCode → bolyraRootNullifier) for attribution-plus-authorization correlation. Enables compliance queries like 'show all payments by root identity X within delegation scope FINANCIAL_MEDIUM in Q3'. Complexity is high because ERC-8021 attribution events and Bolyra nullifiers have different privacy models that need reconciliation.

theseus_integration_surface: Theseus (theseuschain.com) sits between x402 payment facilitators and autonomous agents. Bolyra plugs in at two surfaces. First, the agent-side SDK surface: before an agent submits an x402 402-response, it calls provePaymentAuthorization(delegationChain, {amount, recipient, receiptHash, timestamp}) via @bolyra/sdk, receives a serialized PLONK proof, and attaches it as the X-Bolyra-Payment-Authorization header. Theseus forwards this header alongside the payment. Second, the facilitator-side verifier surface: the x402 facilitator (or Theseus's settlement layer) calls X402AuthorizationVerifier.verifyPaymentAuthorization(receiptHash, proof, publicSignals) on Base Sepolia before accepting the payment as authorized. The verifier contract calls DelegationPlonkVerifier (already deployed), checks IdentityRegistry root history for the claimed Merkle root at payment time T, and emits the PaymentAuthorized event for the audit log. The ERC-8021 bridge optionally correlates the emitted nullifier to Builder Code attribution. Theseus never learns the root human identity or intermediate delegator keys—only the scope mask, nullifier, and expiry timestamp appear as public signals.

Walk through the architecture diagram: Theseus agent key → Bolyra credential → payment auth proof → x402 receipt header → on-chain verifier. Show the deployed DelegationPlonkVerifier on Base Sepolia via Basescan. Present the X-Bolyra-Auth-Proof header spec as a draft and propose co-authoring. risks: Theseus VM is not EVM-compatible: DelegationVerifier.sol cannot deploy directly; need to ship proof bytes off-chain and verify on Base Sepolia instead, adding a cross-chain round-trip that hurts UX and is a harder sell.

Circuit recompile with new public inputs requires regenerating zkeys from pot16.ptau (~20min local, non-parallelizable); if pot16.ptau has wrong constraint capacity the build fails and needs a new ceremony — check constraint count before starting.

Theseus agent key schema is unknown: if agents use something other than Ed25519 (e.g. secp256k1 or a custom VM key type), the AgentPolicy circuit's EdDSA verifier doesn't apply and a new constraint subcircuit is needed (~3 extra days).

Solo founder context-switching cost: Delegation circuit modification requires uninterrupted deep work (Circom constraint debugging is non-linear). Interleaving with ZKProva/GeniusComply tasks will double the real-calendar time.
x402 is not yet stable: the Linux Foundation spec is still in draft; the receipt format may change before Bolyra's header extension ships, requiring a spec update pass.
