The proof generation adds < 5s latency for snarkjs (dev) and < 300ms for rapidsnark (prod). Theseus team can reproduce the demo using the bridge adapter with their own agent keys.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Agent identity schema: exact format of their EdDSA keypairs and any on-chain agent registration struct. 2) Testnet RPC + faucet access if they want the verifier deployed on their chain. 3) Confirmation of VM type (EVM-compatible or custom) — this is the single highest-leverage question for Sunday. 4) x402 facilitator they're planning to integrate with (Base x402, custom) so the receipt format is known. 5) Willingness to co-author the X-Bolyra-Auth-Proof spec as a joint Bolyra+Theseus proposal to the x402 Foundation.

tier3_findings: AXIS 2 FAIL — Theseus's 'no human key path' architecture is a structural kill shot for the primary ZKP value proposition. The entire privacy argument rests on 'hiding root identity X and intermediate delegators from the payment receipt.' But in an agent-native chain where there IS no human root and agents ARE the first-class principals, there is nothing sensitive to hide. An EdDSA delegation chain posted on-chain (agent A signs over to agent B signs over to agent C, all public) provides the same audit trail with zero ZKP overhead. The Bolyra rationale assumes human-rooted privacy is the motivator — Theseus's architecture explicitly eliminates that assumption.

AXIS 4 FAIL — Integration complexity is disqualifying at three independent layers: (1) Theseus is an L1 with unknown VM compatibility — if non-EVM, Bolyra's Solidity verifier is useless and requires a custom Groth16 verifier implementation from scratch in Theseus's native runtime, a multi-month effort for Theseus alone; (2) the Delegation circuit requires 'major' modification to add payment-context binding inputs (amount, recipient, receipt hash), triggering a new trusted setup ceremony and regenerated Solidity verifier — Bolyra estimates 10+ days just for the circuit work; (3) there is no finalized x402 receipt format for ZKP attachment, making this a standards problem in addition to a build problem. Three compounding blockers against a $100K-funded pre-mainnet partner is an unacceptable risk ratio.

AXIS 1 CONCERN — A Theseus engineer with basic crypto literacy can build an on-chain delegation registry (agent → agent signed scoped grants, stored as Merkle leaves, referenced in payment receipts) using standard Solidity + OpenZeppelin in 4-6 weeks. This satisfies 80% of the audit use case without ZKP. The 20% gap is the privacy property — but as noted in Axis 2, that property may have no value in Theseus's architecture. The 'ZKP expertise barrier' argument is only valid if privacy is the requirement, which is unproven.

AXIS 3 CONCERN — Enterprise operators requiring audit-grade authorization proofs on autonomous agent payments will not be deploying on a $100K-funded, pre-mainnet L1 within 12 months. The demand signal comes from x402's backing (Google, Visa, Stripe) — those are enterprise players who will not be on Theseus at any near-term horizon. The actual Theseus agent cohort at launch will be crypto-native developers running experimental agents, not regulated enterprise workflows requiring authorization proofs. The use case is real but arrives at 100K+ agent scale, which Theseus is years from.

AXIS 5 PASS — The x402 payment attribution proof is genuinely reusable across any x402-adopting platform (Fetch.ai, SingularityNET, future agent chains, even Coinbase's own agent ecosystem). If Bolyra builds the Delegation circuit modification with payment-context binding, it becomes a protocol-level primitive that doesn't die with Theseus. The work is worth doing eventually — but not with Theseus as the forcing function at this stage.

COMPOUND FINDING — The proposal conflates two distinct gaps: (a) attribution vs. authorization (real and ERC-8021 confirms it) and (b) privacy of the delegation chain (unproven value in agent-native context). It wins on (a) but loses on (b), and the ZKP overhead is only justified by (b). A non-ZKP authorization proof (EdDSA delegation chain with on-chain verification) closes gap (a) adequately for Theseus's actual use cases. Recommend decoupling: build the simpler EdDSA solution for Theseus as a fast win, reserve ZKP payment proof for a human-rooted operator context where privacy of the delegation chain has provable economic value.
tier3_summary: Axis 2 is a structural kill: Theseus's agent-native, no-human-root architecture eliminates the privacy motivation for ZKP over EdDSA delegation chains, while Axis 4's triple-layer integration complexity (unknown VM, major circuit modification, no x402 ZKP receipt standard) makes this a multi-month build against a pre-mainnet $100K-funded partner — DROP from CONSIDER to REJECT.

title: Agent-rooted Delegation circuit: L1 genesis as root of trust
description: Bolyra's existing Delegation circuit hard-assumes a human delegator — the delegator key is bound to a HumanUniqueness credential commitment. Theseus agents have no human key path; the root of trust is the agent's on-chain genesis transaction. A new AgentDelegation circuit variant is needed where the root delegator is validated against the Theseus L1 genesis block commitment rather than a Semaphore-style human tree root. The existing scope-narrowing logic (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope, expiry monotonicity, max-3-hop chain) carries over with ~3k additional constraints to verify the L1 genesis commitment. This is the single most important architectural gap: without it, Bolyra cannot support any agent-to-agent delegation on Theseus at all.

rationale.agent_need: The structural argument is compelling — if Theseus is truly agent-native L1 with no human key path, the current Delegation circuit literally cannot function as a delegator root — but the evidence relies on training-knowledge characterizations of Theseus rather than cited documentation or codebase, weakening the required evidence standard.
rationale.zkp_edge: Correctly names Lit Protocol (no circuit-level scope narrowing), Turnkey (custodial trusted third party), and Fetch.ai (replay/impersonation gaps) as structural failures, but does not address whether a simple on-chain ACL contract with Merkle inclusion proofs could provide 80% of the value without ZKP overhead.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Names specific circuits (Delegation.circom fork, AgentPolicy), contracts (IdentityRegistry new enrollment path, new verifiers), and a plausible 2-week timeline, but the entire work is gated on a critical external dependency — Theseus must share their genesis block commitment format before a single constraint can be written, making the timeline speculative.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Correctly identifies ZKP circuit design and Circom proficiency as the expertise gap and notes competitor alternatives (Lit, Turnkey) Theseus could adopt instead, but does not assess Theseus's actual crypto team composition or whether they have an identity solution already in progress, leaving the 'they can't build this themselves' claim unsubstantiated.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human-controlled key path; agents hold their own keys and persist on-chain, creating a structural gap with all human-rooted identity systems including Bolyra's current Delegation circuit.

Lit Protocol provides programmable key management and access control for agents using threshold cryptography, but does not use ZKP delegation chains or cumulative-bit permission encoding — no circuit-level scope narrowing.
Turnkey offers API-driven key management with policy enforcement for agents, but is custodial and trust-anchored to Turnkey's infrastructure, not to an L1 genesis commitment.
Privy targets human wallet UX (embedded wallets, social login) and has limited agent-native primitives; not designed for agent-rooted trust chains.

Fetch.ai runs an agent-native network where agents register on-chain and communicate via the Agent Communication Protocol (ACP); delegation is handled via agent-signed messages rather than ZKP circuits, creating exploitable replay/impersonation gaps.
SingularityNET focuses on AI service marketplace mechanics; identity is service-level API-key gating, not cryptographic agent delegation.

evidence.source: IETF draft-klrc-aiagent / OAuth 2.0 for AI agents (training knowledge)
evidence.summary: Emerging IETF drafts on AI agent authorization propose OAuth-style token delegation but assume a human principal at the root — no standard yet covers agent-genesis-rooted delegation chains, which is exactly the gap a Theseus-native AgentDelegation circuit would fill.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 / Worldcoin ZKP identity (training knowledge)
evidence.summary: Semaphore v4 is the most deployed ZKP identity primitive on L1s (Ethereum mainnet), but its Merkle tree root is human-biometric-anchored; no variant exists that substitutes an L1 genesis block commitment as the root of trust.

evidence.source: Aztec / Noir ZKP on L1 (training knowledge)
evidence.summary: Aztec's Noir language enables privacy-preserving circuits on Ethereum, but no published circuit addresses agent-genesis delegation chains; the tooling is available for Bolyra to build this without competition.
Polygon ID supports W3C Verifiable Credentials with ZKP, but is human-credential-centric and does not model agent autonomy or genesis-block trust anchors.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core scope-narrowing logic (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope, expiry monotonicity, max-3-hop chain, delegation nullifier replay protection, EdDSA delegation token signature) carries over verbatim. The circuit template is the starting point for AgentDelegation — only the root-delegator identity check changes.

The root delegator in AgentDelegation is an agent credential, not a human. AgentPolicy's identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp)) and EdDSA operator signature verification become the delegator identity anchor, replacing the HumanUniqueness Merkle root check that the existing Delegation circuit uses.

Not applicable as delegator root. Its Semaphore v4 Merkle tree and nullifier construction are human-specific. However, mixed human→agent delegation chains that terminate at a Theseus agent still need HumanUniqueness for any human-origin hop, so the circuit interface must remain compatible with the existing handshake tuple.

The agentTree (dual LeanIMT, 30-entry root history buffer) already tracks enrolled agent commitments on Base Sepolia. For agent-rooted delegation, the genesis commitment from Theseus L1 must either (a) be mirrored into agentTree via a bridge-attested enrollment, or (b) verified out-of-band before the delegation proof is accepted. The existing delegationVerify path and hop-count enforcement reuse with a new entry point that accepts an agent-root proof instead of a human-root proof.

Cannot reuse directly — the new AgentDelegation circuit will generate a different R1CS/constraint system. A new PLONK verifier contract must be generated from the new zkey. The existing PlonkVerifier address is circuit-specific.

new_components_needed.description: AgentDelegation.circom: fork of Delegation.circom replacing the delegator HumanUniqueness Merkle check with (1) AgentPolicy-style EdDSA operator signature over the root credential commitment, and (2) a Theseus L1 genesis block commitment verifier (~3k additional constraints, likely Poseidon-hashed block header fields or a compact Merkle path into the genesis state trie). Scope-narrowing, expiry, hop-count, and nullifier replay logic copy verbatim. Dual Groth16+PLONK build required for ceremony flexibility.

Theseus Genesis Commitment Interface Spec: define the exact fields from the Theseus L1 genesis block that serve as the root-of-trust anchor (block hash, state root, agent registry commitment), their encoding as ZK-friendly field elements (Baby Jubjub / BN254 scalar field), and the on-chain attestation mechanism (oracle, storage proof, or bridge message). Without this spec the circuit cannot be implemented — this is the critical unknown.
AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier.sol: generated from the new AgentDelegation zkey via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Deploy alongside existing verifiers on Base Sepolia.

IdentityRegistry extension (agentDelegationVerify): new method or overloaded verify path that accepts (agentRootProof, delegationChainProofs[], nonce) instead of (humanProof, agentProof, nonce). Must validate Theseus genesis commitment is in the trusted genesis anchor set (owner-controlled mapping), check hop count ≤ 3, enforce delegation nullifiers, and emit the same events as existing verifyHandshake for indexer compatibility.

Implement delegate() stub for agent-rooted path: createAgentDelegation(rootCredential, delegateeCredential, scopeMask, expiry) → AgentDelegationResult. Wire to AgentDelegation circuit artifacts, add ThesusBridgeAdapter that fetches and caches the genesis commitment from Theseus RPC, expose verifyAgentDelegationChain(). Add AgentDelegationError and GenesisMismatchError to error class hierarchy.

Theseus bridge adapter: lightweight module that reads the Theseus L1 genesis block commitment via Theseus RPC/REST, converts it to BN254 field elements, and caches it with a configurable TTL. Used by the SDK proving path and optionally by the IdentityRegistry oracle updater.

theseus_integration_surface: Two viable surfaces: (A) Verifier-on-Base-Sepolia with oracle bridge — Bolyra's IdentityRegistry stays on Base Sepolia; a trusted oracle (or Theseus→Base bridge message) writes the Theseus L1 genesis commitment into a GenesisAnchorRegistry contract; the AgentDelegation circuit proves membership against this anchored value; no cross-chain proving required. (B) Native Theseus deployment — deploy AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier and a minimal IdentityRegistry fork directly on Theseus L1 so the genesis commitment is a local constant (block.chainid + genesis hash available natively); agents prove delegation entirely on-chain without bridging. Surface A is faster (reuses existing Base Sepolia infra, 2-week sprint feasible) but introduces oracle trust. Surface B is cleaner (genesis commitment is a chain constant, zero oracle risk) but requires Theseus EVM compatibility verification and a new deployment pipeline.

deliverables.name: AgentDelegation spec + Theseus genesis commitment format
deliverables.description: Document the wire format for Theseus L1 genesis block commitment (block hash fields, Poseidon-hashed header, or compact Merkle path). Defines the public signal interface AgentDelegation.circom will consume. Requires Theseus to share genesis block structure. This is the critical dependency that gates all circuit work.

deliverables.name: AgentDelegation.circom
deliverables.description: Fork of Delegation.circom. Replaces the HumanUniqueness Merkle root check with: (1) AgentPolicy-style EdDSA operator signature over the root credential commitment, and (2) Poseidon-hash verification of the Theseus L1 genesis block commitment (~3k additional constraints). Scope-narrowing (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope), expiry monotonicity, max-3-hop chain, and nullifier replay protection copy verbatim. Dual Groth16+PLONK build. Will use pot16.ptau for Groth16 trusted setup — no new ceremony needed if constraint count stays under 2^16.
deliverables.reuses: Delegation.circom (scope-narrowing, expiry, hop-count, nullifier logic — ~70% of constraints)

deliverables.name: AgentDelegationGroth16Verifier.sol + AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier.sol
deliverables.description: Regenerated Solidity verifier contracts from the new AgentDelegation zkey. Cannot reuse existing DelegationPlonkVerifier — different R1CS. Generated via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier from the new vkey.json. Hardhat integration test confirms verifyProof accepts valid witness and rejects tampered scope.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry: agent-root enrollment path
deliverables.description: New entry point on IdentityRegistry that accepts a bridge-attested Theseus genesis commitment and inserts it into the agentTree (existing dual LeanIMT, 30-entry root history buffer). Adds an enrollAgentGenesis(bytes32 genesisCommitment, bytes calldata bridgeAttestation) method. Bridge attestation is initially a multisig (2-of-3 Theseus team keys) pending a trustless bridge. Existing delegationVerify path reused with a flag selecting agent-root vs human-root entry.

deliverables.name: SDK: agentDelegation() API methods
deliverables.description: Two new TypeScript methods on @bolyra/sdk: createAgentDelegation(rootCredential, delegateeCredential, scopeBitmask, expiry, genesisCommitment) and verifyAgentDelegation(proof, nonce). Thin wrappers over snarkjs prover using the new AgentDelegation zkey. Existing proveHandshake/verifyHandshake session-nonce binding reused for the handshake layer.
deliverables.reuses: createAgentCredential, proveHandshake patterns (proving pipeline, nonce binding)

deliverables.name: Theseus integration demo
deliverables.description: Node script demonstrating a 2-hop agent delegation on Base Sepolia testnet: root agent (Theseus genesis commitment enrolled via multisig) → delegatee agent (narrowed scope bitmask) → proof verification on-chain. Shows the score from 76 (CONSIDER) is actionable: scope-narrowing works, replay fails, expired delegation rejected. Requires Theseus testnet genesis commitment or a mock commitment for demo purposes.
deliverables.reuses: examples/mcp-demo pattern + existing Hardhat testnet scripts

spec.demo_description: For the Sunday June 29 call: (1) live demo of the existing Delegation circuit showing scope-narrowing and max-3-hop enforcement — proves the core primitive works; (2) AgentDelegation.circom scaffold open in editor showing the exact lines being changed (the HumanUniqueness Merkle check replaced by EdDSA + genesis hash verify); (3) the spec doc showing the wire format Theseus needs to expose. Nothing deployed yet — but the gap is precisely scoped and the delta from existing code is visibly small. The ask of Theseus: share genesis block Poseidon-hash format so spec can be finalized on the call.
risks: Theseus genesis commitment format is unknown — if they use a non-Poseidon hash (e.g. keccak256 of raw RLP), adding a keccak gadget in Circom adds ~5k constraints and complexity; resolve on the call
pot16.ptau caps at 2^16 constraints — if AgentDelegation exceeds this (unlikely but possible with complex genesis Merkle path), a new Powers of Tau ceremony is needed, adding 2-3 days
Bridge attestation is initially a multisig trust assumption — trustless bridge from Theseus L1 to Base Sepolia is a separate multi-month engineering effort; demo must be honest about this
Solo capacity: circuit work is sequential and debugging Circom witness errors is unpredictable — 8-day estimate for AgentDelegation.circom has ±3 day variance
Theseus testnet may not exist yet — demo may need a mock genesis commitment if their chain is not live
spec.success_criteria: A 2-hop agent delegation proof verifies on-chain (Base Sepolia) with: (a) delegateeScope strictly ⊆ delegatorScope enforced in-circuit, (b) replay of the same proof with a fresh nonce fails, (c) a tampered genesis commitment causes proof verification to reject, (d) SDK round-trip (createAgentDelegation → verifyAgentDelegation) completes in under 30 seconds using snarkjs on a laptop
spec.theseus_dependencies: Critical path item 1: genesis block commitment format — specifically which fields are hashed, what hash function (Poseidon preferred; keccak adds circuit complexity), and byte ordering. Item 2: a testnet or staging environment where agents have on-chain genesis transactions we can use to generate test commitment values. Item 3: a 2-of-3 multisig signing authority for bridge attestations during the demo phase (their team keys). Nice-to-have: their agent credential schema (public key format, on-chain registration record structure) so AgentPolicy's EdDSA signature check binds to real Theseus keypairs.

tier3_findings: AXIS 2 FAIL — Standard crypto suffices for agent delegation: a Solidity ACL contract enforcing delegateePermissions & delegatorPermissions == delegateePermissions (bitwise AND check) enforces the same cumulative-bit scope narrowing the Delegation circuit provides, with nullifier replay protection via a simple spent-nullifier mapping. The privacy benefit of hiding delegation chains is unclear for an agent-native L1 where chain transparency is a feature, not a bug. The proposal never addresses what concrete harm occurs if delegation chains are visible on-chain, which is the only question that would justify ZKP overhead.

AXIS 4 FAIL — Integration is structurally blocked on unknowns that Bolyra cannot resolve before the Sunday demo: Theseus's VM architecture is undocumented ($100K funded, likely pre-mainnet), the genesis block commitment format is unknown, and the EVM-compatibility status is unconfirmed. The proposal itself admits the entire 2-week sprint is gated on Theseus sharing their genesis commitment format first. If Theseus runs a custom non-EVM VM, Solidity verifiers cannot be deployed and Bolyra would need to implement a custom Groth16 verifier in Theseus's native language — work that dwarfs the circuit modification itself. The claimed timeline is fictional until these prerequisites are satisfied.

AXIS 1 CONCERN — Theseus can defer this: for their current scale ($100K, pre-launch), a hardcoded permission set per agent enforced in a Solidity registry is a 1-week implementation. ZKP circuit design requires specialized Circom expertise, but Theseus's identity problem at launch is not complex enough to demand it. The 'they can't build this themselves' claim is unsubstantiated — the proposal acknowledges it doesn't know Theseus's crypto team composition.

AXIS 3 CONCERN — The agent use case is named (agent spawns sub-agent, needs scope-narrowed delegation) but the ZKP-specific version is not necessary at launch. Theseus at launch likely has tens to hundreds of agents, where gas-efficient proof verification is irrelevant and on-chain ACL call overhead is acceptable. The ZKP version only becomes operationally necessary at scale that $100K funding will not reach.

AXIS 5 CONCERN — Partial reuse value exists: the AgentDelegation circuit pattern (agent-genesis as root of trust instead of Semaphore human tree) is generalizable to any agent-native L1. However, the genesis commitment binding is Theseus-specific, and Theseus's failure probability at current funding level is high. The net reusable artifact is roughly 60% of the effort; the Theseus-specific genesis integration work is stranded if they pivot or fold.
tier3_summary: Two hard FAILs — standard Solidity ACLs cover the scope-narrowing claim without ZKP, and the 2-week sprint is entirely blocked on undocumented Theseus VM and genesis format — make this a DROP under the rubric regardless of architectural appeal.

title: EIP-1186 storage proof relay to sync Bolyra IdentityRegistry root onto Theseus L1
description: Bolyra's IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia maintains a Merkle root of enrolled agent credentials. For Theseus agents to be verifiable on Base without a trusted bridge, Theseus L1 needs to pin that Merkle root. Using EIP-1186 Ethereum storage proofs, a light-client relay can submit the IdentityRegistry slot proof to a Theseus L1 contract, updating the pinned root trustlessly. A Bolyra ZKP generated on Base is then verifiable on Theseus by checking inclusion against the locally-synced root — no bridge operator required. This is the key architectural piece that makes Bolyra the canonical cross-chain identity layer rather than a chain-specific system; Theseus cannot easily replicate this without Bolyra's IdentityRegistry being the source of truth on Base.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' structural constraint on Theseus creates an inherent need for trustless cross-chain credential verification, but evidence relies on inferred architecture rather than direct Theseus documentation citing this need.
rationale.zkp_edge: Named alternatives (trusted bridge, multisig, ACL contracts) all require a trusted third party operator, which is a structural failure ZKP+EIP-1186 eliminates, though the storage proof relay is EIP-1186 mechanics rather than ZKP itself, making the ZKP edge specifically about proof portability once the root is pinned.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Specific primitives named (PlonkVerifier, Groth16Verifier, IdentityRegistry root history buffer) are reusable verbatim with zero circuit changes; new work is scoped realistically to BolyraRootPinner.sol admin stub (2 days) with the full trustless relay left as follow-on, which is an honest gap.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The ZKP circuit design and Circom expertise gap is real and well-identified, and IdentityRegistry as source of truth creates bilateral value, but the argument that Theseus cannot build this themselves in 3 months is underdeveloped given no assessment of their team's crypto background or whether competitor solutions (Lit PKP) could serve the same role.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with 'no human-controlled key path' constraint, meaning agents hold keys natively — raw EdDSA keypairs are the assumed current identity primitive, with no ZKP delegation layer described.

Lit offers distributed threshold-signature key management for agent wallets (PKP — Programmable Key Pairs) with Lit Actions for conditional signing, but no ZKP-based cross-chain identity or delegation circuit — operates on access control conditions, not cryptographic proofs of scope.

Turnkey provides non-custodial server-side wallets increasingly used for agent key management, but identity is simply ECDSA/EdDSA key custody with no on-chain registry, Merkle roots, or delegation proofs.

Privy offers embedded + server wallets for agents but is strictly a key-management layer with no cross-chain identity registry, ZKP proofs, or permission encoding — targets Web2 developers, not L1-native agent architectures.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + Verifiable Credentials spec — training knowledge
evidence.summary: W3C DIDs support agent DIDs and VCs support delegation via capability invocation, but the VC model relies on JWT/JSON-LD signatures — not ZKP proofs — and has no native cross-chain Merkle root sync mechanism.

evidence.source: IETF draft-klrc-aiagent (referenced in Bolyra bookmarks context)
evidence.summary: Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication is actively evolving; no settled standard exists for agent-to-agent delegation with cryptographic scope narrowing — this is a spec gap Bolyra can position into.

EIP-1186 (eth_getProof) is a mature Ethereum standard for generating Merkle-Patricia storage proofs; it is production-ready and widely used by light clients (Helios, Nimbus) — the relay mechanism proposed for Bolyra has strong technical precedent.

Fetch.ai has agent identity via its uAgents framework with DID-like addressing, but uses a proprietary ledger and has no ZKP-based credential or permission proof system — cross-chain identity is handled via bridges, not storage proofs.

World ID deploys Semaphore-based ZKP identity on-chain (human-only), proving group membership via nullifiers — structurally analogous to Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit, but entirely human-scoped with no agent credential or delegation layer.

Polygon ID offers ZKP-based verifiable credentials on-chain with on-chain state (identity state tree) and cross-chain state sync via state relay contracts — the closest live analog to Bolyra's proposed IdentityRegistry root sync, but human/VC-centric with no agent permission bit encoding.

Semaphore is deployed on Ethereum mainnet/L2s and proves group membership anonymously — Bolyra reuses its depth-20 ceremony for HumanUniqueness, giving Bolyra ceremony legitimacy; no agent-identity equivalent exists in the Semaphore ecosystem.

Helios demonstrates production-grade EIP-1186 storage proof verification in Rust for trustless Ethereum state reading — confirms the relay architecture is buildable without novel cryptography, only integration work.

mapped_primitives.usage: The source of truth for the relay. The 30-entry root history buffer on agentTree and humanTree provides a natural sync window — a relay submitting EIP-1186 proofs can pin any of the 30 most-recent roots without requiring the relay to be atomic with each Base Sepolia enrollment. The contract's deterministic storage layout (LeanIMT root slot) is the exact slot proven via eth_getProof.

Circuit is chain-agnostic by construction: the public output agentMerkleRoot is a field element that Theseus's pinned root can be checked against directly. A proof generated on Base is valid on Theseus if the verifier there checks the same root. Zero circuit changes needed.

Same chain-agnostic property as AgentPolicy. The scope commitment chain links credential commitments, not chain IDs. Delegation proofs generated on Base are portable to any chain that has the pinned root and the PlonkVerifier deployed.

Existing PlonkVerifier bytecode (0x28b99c48…) can be deployed verbatim on Theseus. AgentPolicy and Delegation proofs from Base are verifiable against the locally-synced root with no code change — only a redeployment.
Existing Groth16Verifier bytecode (0x5467FCbe…) deployed verbatim on Theseus for HumanUniqueness proofs. Enables full handshake verification (human + agent) on Theseus once the roots are pinned.
Redeploy verbatim on Theseus to support on-chain delegation chain verification against the pinned root.

new_components_needed.description: BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol on Theseus L1. Accepts an EIP-1186 proof bundle (account proof + storage proof for the IdentityRegistry agentTree.root slot on Base Sepolia), verifies MPT inclusion against a trusted Base Sepolia block header hash, and writes the proven root to a local pinnedRoot mapping keyed by block number. Emits RootPinned(blockNumber, agentRoot, humanRoot). This is the trust anchor — all Theseus-side ZKP verification gates against this contract.

TheseusBlockHeaderOracle interface / adapter. BolyraStorageProofRelay needs a trusted Base Sepolia block header to verify the storage proof against. If Theseus already runs an Ethereum light client (e.g. Helios-style), this is a thin adapter (~2 days). If not, a committee-signed header feed must be built as an interim measure until a canonical light client exists, which is significantly heavier. Estimate assumes Theseus has a usable block header source.

BolyraVerifierHub.sol on Theseus. Mirrors IdentityRegistry's verifyHandshake and verifyDelegation surface but reads from pinnedRoot (via BolyraStorageProofRelay) rather than a local LeanIMT. Wraps the redeployed Groth16Verifier + PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier. This gives Theseus dApps a single integration point identical to the Base API.

Root relay bot (TypeScript). Watches IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia for RootUpdated events (or polls every N blocks), calls eth_getProof for the agentTree.root and humanTree.root storage slots, and submits the proof bundle to BolyraStorageProofRelay on Theseus. Includes freshness logic to skip redundant submissions when pinnedRoot already reflects the current root.

Cross-chain verification support in @bolyra/sdk. Add optional rootOverride and targetChainId to verifyHandshake options so callers on Theseus can pass the pinnedRoot fetched from BolyraStorageProofRelay. Add verifyHandshakeOnChain(proof, { chainId, relayAddress }) as a typed wrapper. Backward-compatible — existing Base callers unaffected.

EIP-1186 storage slot manifest for IdentityRegistry. Documents the exact storage layout (slot derivation for agentTree.root and humanTree.root in the LeanIMT struct), the ABI encoding of the proof bundle, and the relay API surface. Required for Theseus to implement independent relayers and for conformance testing.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) On-chain — BolyraStorageProofRelay + BolyraVerifierHub deployed on Theseus L1; dApps call verifyHandshake on BolyraVerifierHub with proofs generated on Base, which validates against the locally-pinned root. (2) Off-chain relay — a stateless relay bot (can be run by Bolyra, Theseus, or any permissionless party) that submits EIP-1186 proof bundles to BolyraStorageProofRelay whenever IdentityRegistry root changes; relay submissions are trustless since the contract self-verifies the storage proof. (3) SDK bridge — @bolyra/sdk v0.3 exposes verifyHandshakeOnChain({ chainId: theseusChainId, relayAddress }) so Theseus-native agent frameworks (including Theseus's own MCP tooling) can verify Bolyra credentials with one SDK call, making Bolyra's Base Sepolia registry the canonical cross-chain identity source without requiring any bridge operator.

deliverables.name: Bolyra × Theseus Architecture Spec
deliverables.description: One-page doc + sequence diagram: EIP-1186 relay flow, trust anchors, and proof portability model. Covers BolyraStorageProofRelay contract interface, TheseusBlockHeaderOracle dependency surface, and the 3-step verification path (pin root → submit proof → gate action). Used directly in Sunday call.

deliverables.name: Verifier redeploy on Theseus testnet
deliverables.description: Deploy PlonkVerifier (0x28b99c48), Groth16Verifier (0x5467FCbe), and DelegationPlonkVerifier verbatim onto Theseus testnet using existing Hardhat deploy scripts with a new network config entry. Zero code changes — pure redeployment. Proves Bolyra proofs are chain-portable today.

deliverables.name: BolyraRootPinner.sol (admin stub)
deliverables.description: Minimal Theseus-side contract: owner-settable agentRoot and humanRoot mapping keyed by blockNumber. Emits RootPinned events. No MPT logic — root is set by admin call. Enables end-to-end proof verification demo before the trustless relay is built. Will be replaced by BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol but is production-usable with a trusted relayer as an interim.

deliverables.name: Sunday demo script
deliverables.description: Hardhat task that: (1) generates an AgentPolicy proof on Base Sepolia fork, (2) calls BolyraRootPinner.sol on Theseus testnet with the current IdentityRegistry root, (3) calls PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() on Theseus with the Base-generated proof, (4) asserts true. Single terminal session, no live bridge needed. Shows the key claim: Bolyra proofs generated on Base are valid on Theseus.

deliverables.name: eth_getProof relay script (off-chain)
deliverables.description: Node.js script that calls eth_getProof on Base Sepolia for the IdentityRegistry agentTree.root storage slot, encodes the resulting account + storage proof RLP, and submits it to BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol on Theseus. Uses viem's getProof. Runs as a cron or triggered by IdentityRegistry RootUpdated events via a Base Sepolia websocket subscription.

deliverables.name: BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol
deliverables.description: Core trustless relay contract on Theseus. Accepts EIP-1186 proof bundle (RLP-encoded account proof + storage proof), verifies Merkle Patricia Trie inclusion against a trusted Base Sepolia block header hash sourced from TheseusBlockHeaderOracle, and writes the proven agentTree.root to pinnedRoot[blockNumber]. Uses an existing Solidity MPT library (ethereum-optimism/Solidity-RLP or succinctlabs/telepathy-contracts MerklePatricia verifier) to avoid building MPT from scratch. High complexity — storage slot must be computed deterministically from IdentityRegistry's LeanIMT layout.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry storage layout, existing Solidity MPT library

deliverables.name: TheseusBlockHeaderOracle adapter
deliverables.description: Adapter between BolyraStorageProofRelay and Theseus's block header source. Three possible implementations depending on what Theseus provides: (A) if Theseus runs Helios-style light client, wrap its getBlockHash interface; (B) if Theseus has a block hash registry, read from it; (C) if neither, deploy a minimal trusted oracle (multisig-gated) as a placeholder. Complexity and days depend heavily on Theseus testnet access and docs — this is the critical external dependency.
deliverables.reuses: new — shape determined by Theseus architecture

deliverables.name: SDK relay helper in @bolyra/sdk
deliverables.description: Add relayRootToTheseus(theseusRpcUrl, blockTag?) to the TS SDK. Fetches the storage proof, encodes it, and submits to BolyraStorageProofRelay on Theseus. Companion verifyOnTheseus(proof, theseusRpcUrl) checks pinnedRoot and calls the deployed verifier. Lets integrators do the full cross-chain flow in 2 SDK calls.

deliverables.name: Integration test suite
deliverables.description: Hardhat fork-based tests: fork Base Sepolia + simulate Theseus with local Hardhat. Generate real AgentPolicy proof, run relay script, verify against pinned root. Tests the full happy path and three failure cases: stale root (outside 30-block buffer), tampered storage proof, wrong slot. CI-runnable without live testnets.
deliverables.reuses: existing circuit test harness, Hardhat fork config

spec.demo_description: Terminal demo: generate an AgentPolicy PLONK proof on a Base Sepolia fork, manually pin the IdentityRegistry agentTree.root to BolyraRootPinner.sol on Theseus testnet (or local Hardhat), call PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() on Theseus with the Base-generated proof, get true. No bridge, no operator. The ZKP is the bridge. Slides show the trustless relay architecture (EIP-1186 path) as the 3-month roadmap. The claim: circuits are chain-agnostic today, the relay is the 6-week build.
risks: TheseusBlockHeaderOracle: if Theseus has no Ethereum light client or block hash oracle, the trustless relay requires Theseus to build infrastructure first — solo founder cannot unblock this. Interim: trusted admin relay with multisig, delivered in week 2.

MPT proof verification gas cost on Theseus: EIP-1186 Merkle Patricia proofs are 200-400KB RLP and expensive to verify on-chain. If Theseus gas limits are low or the VM is not EVM-equivalent, the relay contract may not be feasible — fallback is an optimistic relay with fraud proofs.
Theseus testnet availability: if testnet is not publicly accessible before the call, the Sunday demo runs on local Hardhat only — sufficient to prove the concept but not live cross-chain.
IdentityRegistry storage slot stability: LeanIMT internal storage layout must be pinned — any contract upgrade that shifts the root slot breaks the relay. Add a slot-stability CI check.
Solo founder sequencing: BolyraStorageProofRelay.sol is 18 days of focused Solidity work. Any parallel SDK or other product obligations push total wall time past 8 weeks.

Theseus EVM compatibility: if Theseus uses a custom VM (not EVM-equivalent), PlonkVerifier bytecode redeploy fails and the verifier must be reimplemented — changes reuse percentage from 65% to under 30%.
spec.success_criteria: A proof generated by @bolyra/sdk on Base Sepolia (or its fork) passes PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() on Theseus L1 against a root that was pinned by BolyraStorageProofRelay without any trusted intermediary signing the root — only the EIP-1186 storage proof and the Theseus block header oracle as trust anchor. Measured by: relay script submits proof → RootPinned event emitted → verifyProof returns true → integration test green in CI.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Testnet RPC endpoint + faucet for contract deployment and gas. 2) Confirmation that Theseus is EVM-compatible (precompiles, opcode parity) — PlonkVerifier uses bn254 pairing precompile (EIP-197). 3) Block header oracle surface: does Theseus expose Base Sepolia block hashes on-chain via a light client, a block hash registry, or neither? This determines relay architecture. 4) VM gas limits: EIP-1186 proof verification is ~2-4M gas; need confirmation Theseus block gas limit accommodates it. 5) If Theseus has existing agent identity contracts, their ABIs — to wire Bolyra root pinning as a gating condition on existing agent registration flows.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: The storage proof relay requires zero ZKP expertise. EIP-1186 is a mature standard with production Rust implementations (Helios, Nimbus). The Bolyra-specific work is identifying the IdentityRegistry storage slot — that's one day of effort. Theseus's engineers can build a functional MPT-based root relay in 6–8 weeks using open-source tooling. The proposal itself concedes 'the storage proof relay is EIP-1186 mechanics rather than ZKP itself,' which means Bolyra's moat is absent from the primary deliverable.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: The trustless property of EIP-1186 is Merkle Patricia Trie verification, not ZKP. Standard crypto (MPT proofs + EdDSA oracle signatures) fully serves the root-pinning function. ZKP only enters at the credential presentation layer, which is a separate concern. At Theseus's current validator count ($100K funding, pre-mainnet), a 2-of-3 multisig oracle updating the pinned root is operationally equivalent and carries near-zero integration overhead. The ZKP overhead is not justified by the marginal trustlessness benefit at this stage.

AXIS 3 — FAIL: No concrete agent use case requiring cross-chain root sync exists at Theseus's current scale. Theseus has essentially zero live agents today. At this stage, agents can directly query Base Sepolia RPC for credential verification with sub-second latency — cross-chain root pinning is a throughput optimization relevant at thousands of simultaneous agent verifications per second. This proposal solves a scaling problem Theseus will not face for at minimum 18–24 months. Kill signal triggered: the need only arises at scale that far exceeds any plausible Theseus 12-month roadmap.

AXIS 4 — CONCERN: If Theseus is non-EVM, deploying an MPT proof verifier from scratch in their native VM is substantially harder than deploying a Groth16 verifier. RLP decoding + Merkle Patricia Trie traversal on-chain is multi-KB proof size with significant compute cost. The Helios evidence confirms buildability on EVM but not on a custom L1 VM. Integration friction may equal or exceed the value delivered, especially since the relay infrastructure must be maintained continuously to re-pin roots as the IdentityRegistry updates.

AXIS 5 — PASS: The EIP-1186 relay architecture generalizes cleanly to any chain that wants to consume Bolyra's IdentityRegistry from Base Sepolia. BolyraRootPinner.sol and the relay daemon are reusable primitives. Building this for Theseus accelerates Bolyra's cross-chain positioning regardless of Theseus's outcome. This is the one axis that holds.
tier3_summary: The relay is EIP-1186 mechanics with no ZKP moat, Theseus has no live agents that need cross-chain verification, and the core trustless property can be approximated with a multisig oracle at zero ZKP cost — REJECT per the 2-or-fewer axis rule.

title: Nullifier-Based Cross-Chain Agent Identity for x402 Micropayments
description: Theseus agents need to interact with Base and Ethereum contracts (DeFi, x402 payment endpoints, Coinbase Agentic Wallets). Revealing the Theseus-native key on Base links all cross-chain activity back to the agent's L1 identity — a privacy and security risk (once the Base key is compromised, the Theseus identity is deanonymized). ZKP nullifiers allow a Theseus agent to prove 'I am the same authorized entity' on Base without revealing its Theseus public key or linking individual transactions. Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit already implements nullifier-hash binding; an agent-rooted variant makes this work without the human Merkle tree. The @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 implementation provides the payment layer. This is the only approach that achieves cross-chain agent pseudonymity — EdDSA + Merkle alone leaks the linkage.

rationale.agent_need: The structural argument that Theseus agents holding EdDSA keypairs face cross-chain deanonymization is sound, but actual Theseus documentation or community discussion confirming this as a pain point was not fetched — evidence remains inferred from the opportunity brief itself.
rationale.zkp_edge: The comparison against Lit Protocol and Turnkey is concrete and structural (deterministic public key linkage persists with MPC/TEE approaches), and the nullifier domain separation argument is technically valid, though the claim that EdDSA+Merkle 'leaks linkage' needs clarification since Merkle proofs can also hide the leaf identity.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy and HumanUniqueness provide reusable building blocks (EdDSA verify, nullifier pattern), but the integration spec itself acknowledges 'major' modifications to both circuits are needed, and a new AgentNullifier.circom plus a new registry contract constitute meaningful new work that warrants more than a 4+2 day estimate.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build ZKP nullifier circuits in-house is plausible given Circom expertise barriers, but no evidence is presented about Theseus's actual team composition, roadmap, or whether they are already evaluating Lit/Turnkey for cross-chain identity.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (not fetched — web search denied; inferred from provided opportunity brief)
evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human-controlled key path; agents hold their own EdDSA keypairs, making raw key linkage across chains the default (and a structural deanonymization risk) without a ZKP nullifier layer.

Lit provides threshold-key MPC for agent wallets, granting cross-chain signing without exposing a single private key, but does not implement ZKP nullifiers for unlinkable pseudonymity — it still reveals a deterministic public key per chain.
Turnkey offers TEE-backed key custody for agents; like Lit it protects the key but does not decouple on-chain identity from the underlying keypair, so cross-chain activity remains linkable.
Privy targets human-owned wallets with embedded wallet UX; minimal native support for headless agent-to-agent flows or ZKP-based credential delegation.

evidence.source: W3C Verifiable Credentials + DID-Linked Resources specs — general knowledge
evidence.summary: VC/DID standards provide a substrate for agent credential issuance but lack ZKP nullifier binding for cross-chain pseudonymity; they rely on presenter-controlled selective disclosure, not circuit-enforced unlinkability.

evidence.source: IETF draft-klrc-aiagent-auth (inferred from bolyra/CLAUDE.md reference to spec/draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md)
evidence.summary: Emerging IETF agent-auth drafts discuss credential scoping and delegation chains but do not specify nullifier-based cross-chain unlinkability — a gap Bolyra's circuit design directly addresses.
Fetch.ai has agent-native on-chain identity via uAgents but uses ECDSA keypairs with no ZKP layer; cross-chain pseudonymity is not a design goal and agents are linkable across interactions.
SingularityNET focuses on AI service marketplace coordination, not privacy-preserving agent identity; no ZKP nullifier mechanism present.

evidence.source: Aztec Network / noir identity patterns — general knowledge
evidence.summary: Aztec's ZKP-based account model demonstrates nullifier-per-action patterns in production on L1-adjacent rollups, validating technical feasibility of agent nullifier binding at chain scale.

Semaphore's production nullifier-hash circuit (already reused in Bolyra HumanUniqueness) proves the primitive is ceremony-ready and audited; an agent-rooted variant skipping the human Merkle tree is a well-scoped engineering delta.

evidence.source: Coinbase Agentic Wallets launch blog (provided signal URL)
evidence.summary: Coinbase's agentic wallet SDK explicitly targets agent-controlled Base transactions — confirming the Base payment rail Theseus agents will hit, and therefore the concrete deanonymization risk from key linkage across chains.

evidence.source: AWS x402 agentic commerce blog (provided signal URL)
evidence.summary: AWS endorsement of x402 as the agentic micropayment standard signals wide adoption, raising the stakes for privacy in x402 payment flows where per-payment key reuse enables behavioral fingerprinting.

mapped_primitives.usage: The Poseidon2(scope, secret) nullifier construction is the direct template for an agent-rooted nullifier. The nullifier domain separation pattern (scope-keyed, unlinkable across scopes) and nonce binding for replay protection transfer directly. However the identity anchor is currently the human Semaphore secret + humanTree membership, both of which are irrelevant for agents.

The credential commitment — Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp) — is the right identity anchor for an agent nullifier. The EdDSA operator signature verification and agentTree Merkle membership proof establish that the credential is legitimate before a nullifier is derived. These two building blocks are directly reusable. The circuit currently lacks a nullifier output signal.

Sub-agent delegation chains apply when a Theseus orchestrator spawns child agents that independently interact with x402 endpoints. The scope-narrowing and expiry-narrowing guarantees ensure a child agent cannot claim broader cross-chain permissions than the parent. The hop-count ceiling (max 3) bounds delegation depth. No structural modification needed; chain-ID binding in the scope commitment would need to be threaded through.

The agentTree (LeanIMT depth 20, 30-entry root history buffer) on Base Sepolia is the on-chain trust anchor. Agent credentials registered here are the Merkle leaves whose membership the new AgentNullifier circuit will prove. The dual-tree architecture and root history buffer (supports ~10-minute liveness windows) carry over. The nullifier replay-protection pattern (delegationNullifiers mapping) is the direct model for a cross-chain nullifier registry.

The @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 implementation provides the payment transport layer. The HTTP 402 + Authorization header pattern is the integration surface where a nullifier proof would replace or augment the current credential attestation — proving 'I am an authorized agent' to the x402 endpoint without revealing the Theseus key or linking payments.

An AgentNullifier circuit targeting Groth16 (like HumanUniqueness, ~16K constraints) would reuse the existing deployed Groth16Verifier on Base Sepolia, avoiding a new verifier deploy for the first iteration. PLONK would require a new verifier.

new_components_needed.description: AgentNullifier.circom — agent-rooted nullifier circuit. Identity anchor: credentialCommitment (from AgentPolicy). Public outputs: nullifierHash = Poseidon2(chainId ++ scope, credentialCommitment), scopeCommitment, chainIdBinding. Private inputs: credentialCommitment, permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp, agentTreePathElements[20], agentTreePathIndices[20], operatorSignature. Proves: (1) Merkle inclusion in agentTree, (2) operator EdDSA signature valid, (3) scope bits satisfied, (4) expiry > currentTimestamp, (5) nullifier correctly derived. Strips human Merkle tree entirely. Chain-ID enters nullifier domain so the same credential produces unlinkable nullifiers on Base vs Ethereum mainnet.

AgentNullifierRegistry.sol — extend or deploy alongside IdentityRegistry on Base. Tracks spent nullifiers per (chainId, scope) pair. Exposes verifyAndSpend(proof, nullifierHash, chainId, scope) and a read-only isSpent(nullifierHash) view. Uses the existing Groth16Verifier. Enforces that the agentTree root in the proof matches a stored root-history entry (liveness window). Emits NullifierSpent(nullifierHash, chainId, scope, timestamp) for off-chain indexing.

proveAgentNullifier(credential, chainId, scope, options) → {proof, nullifierHash, scopeCommitment} added to @bolyra/sdk. Mirrors proveHandshake signature. Fetches live agentTree root from IdentityRegistry, builds witness, runs Groth16 proving via rapidsnark bridge. Companion verifyAgentNullifier() calls AgentNullifierRegistry on-chain.

x402-nullifier middleware for @bolyra/payment-protocols. Intercepts outbound x402 Authorization headers and injects a Bolyra-Nullifier-Proof field containing the serialized AgentNullifier proof. Payment endpoint SDK (server-side) calls verifyAgentNullifier before settling. Theseus agents configure once via BolyraX402Adapter(credential, chainId) — subsequent .pay() calls are proof-transparent.

Chain-ID nullifier domain separation spec — extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with a new section formalizing nullifier = Poseidon2(Poseidon2(chainId, scope), credentialCommitment). Defines canonical chainId encoding (uint64, big-endian), scope string hashing, and cross-chain replay prevention guarantees. Add 8-10 new conformance test vectors to test-vectors.json covering Base, Ethereum mainnet, and a simulated Theseus L1 chain.

theseus_integration_surface: Bolyra connects to Theseus at three layers. (1) On-chain: AgentNullifierRegistry deployed on Base Sepolia (and later Base mainnet) exposes a single verifyAndSpend entry point. Theseus agent wallets or x402 payment contracts call this to gate actions on proven identity without learning the Theseus public key. The agentTree root anchored in IdentityRegistry is the trust root — Theseus operators register agent credentials there once. (2) SDK: Theseus agent runtimes import @bolyra/sdk and call proveAgentNullifier(credential, chainId='base', scope='x402:pay') before each micropayment. The proof is ~200 bytes (Groth16), generated in ~0.6s via rapidsnark. (3) x402 payment layer: the BolyraX402Adapter middleware slots into the existing @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 stack. Payment endpoint operators verify the Bolyra-Nullifier-Proof header server-side with a single SDK call — no changes to their core payment logic. Coinbase Agentic Wallet integration would additionally expose the nullifierHash as the stable pseudonymous wallet identifier, replacing the raw Theseus public key in wallet lookup flows.

deliverables.name: AgentNullifier.circom
deliverables.description: New Circom 2 circuit. Anchors identity in Poseidon3(credentialCommitment, chainId, scope) — no human Merkle tree. Outputs nullifierHash = Poseidon2(anchorHash, sessionNonce). Reuses AgentPolicy EdDSA operator signature verification and agentTree membership proof verbatim; adds chainId as a public input and nullifierHash as a new public output signal. Targets Groth16 at ~16K constraints to reuse the existing deployed Groth16Verifier on Base Sepolia.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy (EdDSA verify + agentTree Merkle path), HumanUniqueness (nullifier domain separation + nonce binding pattern)

deliverables.name: AgentNullifierRegistry.sol
deliverables.description: Minimal Solidity contract on Base Sepolia. Maps keccak256(chainId, scope) => nullifierHash => bool. Exposes verifyAndConsume(proof, publicSignals) — calls the existing Groth16Verifier, reverts on replay. Root history buffer (30 entries, ~10-minute liveness window) copied from IdentityRegistry. No new verifier deploy needed.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry (root history buffer, delegationNullifiers mapping pattern), existing deployed Groth16Verifier

deliverables.name: AgentNullifier SDK wrapper
deliverables.description: Adds proveAgentNullifier(credential, chainId, scope, sessionNonce) and verifyAgentNullifier(proof, registry) to @bolyra/sdk. Thin wrapper: generates witness via snarkjs, calls AgentNullifierRegistry.verifyAndConsume on-chain. No new proving logic — reuses existing snarkjs prove/verify pipeline from AgentPolicy.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy SDK prove/verify pipeline, payment-protocols x402 credential injection point

deliverables.name: x402 nullifier proof integration
deliverables.description: Patches @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 handler to attach a ZKP nullifier proof in the Authorization header alongside the credential attestation. Payment endpoint receives proof; calls AgentNullifierRegistry.verifyAndConsume before processing payment. Existing x402 HTTP 402 flow unchanged — nullifier is additive.
deliverables.reuses: payment-protocols x402 handler, AgentNullifier SDK wrapper

deliverables.name: Theseus integration spec
deliverables.description: One-page technical spec + sequence diagram: (1) Theseus agent registers EdDSA credential in Bolyra agentTree on Base Sepolia. (2) Agent computes AgentNullifier proof scoped to chainId=Base + scope=x402-endpoint. (3) Proof replaces raw key in x402 Authorization header. (4) AgentNullifierRegistry consumes nullifier — Theseus L1 identity never appears on Base. Covers: scope encoding, chain ID binding, delegation sub-agent flows, replay protection guarantees.
deliverables.reuses: Bolyra DID method spec, draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md structure

deliverables.name: Sunday demo script
deliverables.description: Runnable Node script showing: (a) existing AgentPolicy circuit generating a credential proof — proves Bolyra can already anchor agent identity; (b) existing x402 payment flow completing against a mock endpoint — proves the payment layer works; (c) hardcoded mock nullifier output where AgentNullifier.circom will plug in — marks the gap clearly. No new circuit needed for Sunday — demo is 70% live code, 30% annotated stub.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy test harness, payment-protocols x402 mock server, existing circuits/test/ scaffolding

spec.demo_description: Run the existing AgentPolicy witness generation (proves agent credential anchoring works today), walk through the x402 payment flow against a local mock server (proves payment layer is live), then show the spec diagram explaining exactly where AgentNullifier.circom slots in to replace the raw key reference. The circuit stub with correct signal declarations can be shown in an editor. The narrative: 'Every piece except one circuit is built — here is what we ship in 2 weeks.'
risks: Theseus VM compatibility unknown: if Theseus L1 is not EVM-compatible, verifyAndConsume cannot be called natively on Theseus — integration would be Base-only (Theseus agent registers on Base, transacts on Base, Theseus L1 is only the key origin). Clarify on Sunday call.

AgentNullifier circuit trusted setup: pot16.ptau is already available, but a new Groth16 zkey requires a phase-2 ceremony. For a 2-week prototype, use a dev-only zkey with no ceremony — flag clearly as non-production.

Solo-founder bandwidth: 12 days of focused circuit + contract + SDK work is tight alongside discovery-autoresearch and differentiation-autoresearch loops still running. Pause autoresearch crons during sprint.
Theseus testnet access: integration tests against real Theseus agents require testnet RPC + funded agent keypairs. Without this, e2e test is Base-only mock — real Theseus agent behavior unverified.

Scope creep from delegation chains: sub-agent x402 flows (Delegation circuit chain-ID threading) are scoped as minor but will surface edge cases. Defer to month-2 unless Theseus explicitly needs it at launch.
spec.success_criteria: A Theseus-origin agent completes an x402 micropayment to a Base Sepolia endpoint where: (1) no Theseus EdDSA public key appears in any Base transaction calldata, (2) the AgentNullifierRegistry rejects a replayed proof for the same (chainId, scope, nonce) tuple, (3) a different scope for the same agent produces an unlinkable nullifier hash (verified by Poseidon2 output diff), and (4) the full prove+verify round trip completes in under 3 seconds using the existing snarkjs pipeline.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Testnet RPC endpoint + chain ID for Theseus L1; sample agent keypair (EdDSA, likely Ed25519 or BabyJubJub — need to confirm curve to verify AgentPolicy compatibility); confirmation of EVM vs custom VM on Theseus (determines whether AgentNullifierRegistry can be deployed natively or remains Base-only); any existing agent credential schema (JSON format, field names) so AgentPolicy credentialCommitment encoding aligns; and confirmation of whether Theseus has an existing on-chain registry Bolyra should integrate with or replace.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 (CONCERN): ZKP circuit expertise is a real barrier — AgentNullifier.circom cannot be built in 2 weeks — but BIP32/BIP44-style HD wallet key derivation lets Theseus agents derive chain-specific keypairs from a master key in a single afternoon. This is already standard practice in multi-chain wallets (MetaMask, Coinbase Wallet) and achieves the pseudonymity goal with zero ZKP expertise required. The proposal conflates 'cross-chain pseudonymity' with 'ZKP nullifiers' without ruling out the obvious alternative.

AXIS 2 (FAIL): The stated use case is x402 micropayments on Base. For a payment to succeed, the agent needs funds and a valid signature — not a cross-chain identity proof. HD key derivation already gives each agent an unlinkable Base key. The nullifier only adds value if a Base contract needs to verify Theseus-level permissions before accepting payment, which is not described anywhere in the integration card. The proposal claims 'EdDSA+Merkle leaks linkage' but Tier 2 review correctly flagged this: Merkle proofs can also hide leaf identity, and separate derived keys have no linkage at all. The core ZKP value proposition for this specific use case evaporates under scrutiny.

AXIS 3 (FAIL): No specific Theseus agent use case is named where the agent operationally FAILS without ZKP nullifiers. The deanonymization risk is theoretical — agents can simply use chain-specific keys. For the first 12 months, Theseus at $100K funding will have dozens of agents at most, all likely controlled by the same small team, where cross-chain behavioral fingerprinting is not a meaningful attack surface. The x402 micropayment use case is purely about payment authorization, not identity continuity. The need for ZKP-based cross-chain pseudonymity only materializes at adversarial scale (thousands of agents with sophisticated chain-analysis opponents) that Theseus cannot reach in 12 months.

AXIS 4 (FAIL): x402 is designed as a low-friction, high-throughput micropayment rail. ZKP proof generation (snarkjs: 3-10 seconds per proof; rapidsnark: 200-500ms) adds latency to every cross-chain payment. This is architecturally backwards — you are adding the heaviest cryptographic primitive to the most latency-sensitive operation. Additionally, Theseus's VM type is unknown; if non-EVM, deploying a Groth16 or PLONK Solidity verifier requires either EVM compatibility work or a custom verifier implementation from scratch. The integration card estimates '4+2 days' for 'major modifications to both circuits' plus a new AgentNullifier.circom and registry contract — this is a material underestimate, and the latency penalty for micropayments alone is disqualifying.

AXIS 5 (PASS WITH CONCERN): The agent-rooted nullifier primitive generalizes to Ritual, Autonolas, and any future agent-native chain. The circuit work has standalone roadmap value for Bolyra regardless of Theseus's outcome. However, Theseus at $100K from Orange DAO is pre-product, pre-mainnet, and pre-revenue. Spending Bolyra engineering time on Theseus-specific integration before Theseus has proven it can ship a chain is a high-risk allocation. The reusable primitives argument partially rescues this axis but does not rescue the overall proposal.
tier3_summary: The proposal conflates cross-chain pseudonymity (solved by HD key derivation in an afternoon) with ZKP-based cross-chain credential portability (a real but entirely different problem), and attaches the heaviest cryptographic primitive to the most latency-sensitive operation in the stack — three separate axes fail, triggering DROP per rubric.
